 You can also add conditions to the environment variables for property comparisons to perform an expansion. For example, you can traverse from the name of the user to their organization using the variable ENV.User-
>SPFUserOrganization_12.Name.
Add a new item to the Quick Find menu
To add a new item to the Quick Find menu.
1. Click File > New > Administration > Quick Find Item in the Desktop Client. The New Quick Find Item form is displayed.
2. Type a Name and Description for the item.
3. In the Display As box, type the text that will be displayed on the Quick Find menu.
4. In the Method box, select the method to be run when this item is selected on the Quick Find menu.
5. In the Menu Sequence box, type the sequence of this item on the Quick Find menu.
6. Click Finish to create the item.
The method must have an access group.
Selecting an owning group will restrict who can access item. See Owning group configuration for more information.
Configure launch URL client APIs
There are two delivered client APIs that can be used to allow users to launch URLs from the software, LaunchURL and LaunchURLWithDyanamicArgs.
LaunchURL
The LaunchURL client API has two arguments and can be used to launch a URL from a menu, for example, to a document or a website.
1. ASP page - This argument can be the path name of a page or the full URL address. For example:
http://hexagonppm.com/products/information-management-product-family
http://hexagonppm.com/ppm-support
 A partial URL is prefixed with the server connection type (http or https), then the web host and web directory.
2. Display mode - This argument sets how the URL displays.
OPEN - the URL opens in a separate window.
FRAME - the URL opens in a client internal frame.
The create and query OBIDs, SessionId, and Username are then appended. For example, the final LaunchURL would be:
http[s]://[myURL]?OBID=[XXX],&sessionid=[YYY]&username=[NAME]
LaunchURLWithDynamicArgs
Like the LaunchURL API, the LaunchURLWithDynamicArgs client API also has two arguments. However, the first argument for this API can use properties from the context object, the related object, or from a set of environment type
variables as constants in the URL string. These constants are substituted at runtime for actual values. This can be used to launch a URL, such as from a menu to an external or internal specific software system, a document, or a website.
1. URL - This argument can be the path name of a page or the full URL. For example:
http://hexagonppm.com/products/information-management-product-family?user=~ENV.Username~&area=~OLaunchURLbj->TagArea_12.Name~
 The URL supports any valid protocol prefix, such as http, https, ftp, and mailto. However, if you wish to use relative URLs that launch pages in the SmartPlant Foundation site, you must prefix the Arg1 value with the
Env.ServerURL variable.
Valid constants that can be appended to the argument of the method include the properties in the following syntax:
Obj.[any property on the object.]
Obj->[PathDefn.Property] – PathDefn can be an edge definition or a relationship definition. For example,
Obj->SPFItemOwningGroup_12.Name or Obj->EDG_EdgeName.Name.
 For more information on condition syntax, see Condition syntax.
Additionally, the argument can use the following ENV. constants:
.SESSIONID
.CONFIGOBID
.USER, .USERNAME
.USERSACTUALNAME
.SERVERURL
.HOSTNAME
.VIRTUALDIRECTORY
.CONFIGCREATE
.CALLEDFROMDIALOG
.CONFIGLEVEL
.QUERYCONFIGLEVEL
.QUERYCONFIGUIDS
.CREATECONFIGUID
.QUERYCONFIGSTRINGS
.CREATECONFIGSTRING
.CURRCONFIGLEVELNAME
EFFECTIVITYDATE
USERORGANIZATIONNAME
USERDEFAULTCREATECONFIG
USERUSERTYPE
USERUSERGROUPS
CONFIG1 to CONFIG5
QUERYCONFIG1 to QUERYCONFIG5
OWNINGGROUPSFORCREATECONFIG
OWNINGGROUPSFORQUERYCONFIG
OWNINGGROUPSFORUSERINCREATECONFIG
OWNINGGROUPSFORUSERINQUERYCONFIG
ACCESSGROUPSFORUSERINCREATECONFIG
ACCESSGROUPSFORUSERINQUERYCONFIG
ALLDOMAINSFORUSER
 Ensure a tilde character is used before and after the constant, so it is correctly recognized.
2. Display mode - This argument sets how the URL displays.
OPEN - the URL opens in a separate window.
FRAME - the URL opens in a client internal frame.
Configure property definition URLs
You can configure a property definition scope as a URL type and build variables into the URL. The variables reference data values directly on the object and reference data values on related objects. The URL property definition value is a
navigable link, which launches a URL from the detail forms, the properties grid, and the list views in the Desktop Client, as well as from the list views in Business Intelligence. The URL property definition also supports environment
variables.
Creating a property definition URL
Using established conventions, such as those used for conditions syntax, you can create variables in a property definition URL. For more information, see Condition syntax.
Edge definitions are not supported since only a single relationship definition hop is supported from any URL property.
Multiple variables can be included in the URL if each traverses a single relationship definition hop. These can be in conjunction with one or more local properties and environment variables, provided each variable meets syntax rules.
Multiple values can be found if an object (such as a tag) belongs to many documents. For example, a URL property definition using a document relationship definition to point to the tag to pick up the Name property. In this example, a
comma-separated set of values is added to the URL in place of the variable.
Variable examples
Variables are constructed similarly for the Desktop Client and for the Web Client. Variables must be wrapped with tildes (~).
Starting object properties
When you want a variable to display the value of a property definition from the starting object (native object), it should be constructed as follows:
~Obj.<Property Definition>~
For example:
~Obj.Name~
Properties from related objects
When you want a variable to display the value of a related object, it should be constructed as follows:
~Obj-><Relationship Definition>_<Direction>.<Property Definition>~
For example:
~Obj->DEVDocumentTag_21.Name~
Environment variables
When you want a variable to display a supported environment variable, it should be constructed as follows:
~ENV.<Variable>~
For example:
~ENV.HOSTNAME~
List view
When the URL is displayed in a list view, it is shown as a navigable link. When the link is clicked, the variables are resolved and the URL navigates to the value. The URL is shown as plain text to help performance. For example:
http://www.livelink.com/intergraph/~Obj.Name~
 The URL does not show the full name of a document, such as http://www.livelink.com/intergraph/C056-AA-ELE-05836-A.
Properties grid
When the URL property is displayed in the properties grid, it will be translated to include the derived values in place of the variables. You can review the URL before it is executed to resolve any limitations with the properties grid when the
related object is accessed during click events.
Forms
When the URL property is displayed in the Query, Update, Create and Copy forms, it is shown inside of a text box so that you can edit and enter any text.
The URL property in the Details form is shown as a navigable link with any variables un-translated. This ensures that the URL property's appearance is consistent across all form types.
 No variables embedded in the URL will be translated for these forms.
Business Intelligence
When a URL property is displayed in a list view in Business Intelligence, it is shown as a navigable link with any variables in their raw, un-translated state.
Relationship management
Shortcut menu relationship menu and relationship drag-and-drop functionality is controlled by relating access groups to relationship definitions. Use Manage Access Groups on the relationship definition. For more information, see Access
group configuration for details.
As an alternative to relationship definition expansion, edge definitions can be created. See Create an Edge Definition for details.
Expand structure
The client API ExpandStructure allows the user to display the structure of a selected object. It can be found on forms, class definitions, and transmittals.
The ExpandStructure client API uses the following methods to expand:
Item to expand
Method
Class definitions
ExpandClassDef
Forms
ExpandForm
Sectioned transmittals
MTH_NonSectionedXtmlHistory
Non-sectioned transmittals
MTH_SectionedXtmlHistory
Sectioned transmittal history
MTH_XtmlExpandNonSectionedStructure
Non-sectioned transmittal history
MTH_XtmlExpandSectionedStructure
For details about the ExpandStructure client API, see ExpandStructure client API.
Example of expanded structure for a class definition
1. Select the class definition in the Desktop Client (SPFFolder is used in this example).
2. Right-click the class definition, and click Expand ClassDef on the shortcut menu.
The ClassDef Structure dialog box is displayed below.
To expand a form structure, right-click the form, and click Expand Form Structure on the shortcut menu.
To expand a transmittal structure, right-click the transmittal, and then click Edit > Expand Structure on the shortcut menu.
Configure column sets
Column sets
Column sets control what properties are displayed in the list view window. Using column sets and column items, administrators can determine the appearance and order of properties to display. Each type of item can have its own column
set to determine which properties appear in the list view window.
Column sets also control which properties are displayed in the tree view. See Tree view display column set (Modified in an update) for more information.
Column items
Column sets are composed of column items. Each column item represents a property or relationship value for an item. You can control the order in which the properties are displayed, the property names that are displayed, column widths,
and other display attributes by setting the values on the column items. Use the Manage Column Items dialog box to create and configure column items.
How column sets work
List view window
Manage related items support
To identify properties and relationships on relationships, set the dynamic flag to TRUE.
Tree view display column set
By default, the tree view displays name and description. You can toggle the display of the description with the Show description in tree user preference. See Change general user preferences for more information.
Sometimes there is a need for additional properties; this is configured by creating a column set and relating it to the class definition to be displayed. Create the relationship by dragging the column set onto the class definition.
For example, the SPFDocumentVersionTreeDisplay column set controls which properties are displayed in the tree view for documents. SPFDocumentVersionTreeDisplay is related to the design document version and template document
version class definitions by the SPFClassDefDisplayColumnSet relationship definition. The following properties are included in the column set by default.
Name
Revision
Version
Workflow Status
Column set resolution
When the Desktop Client displays data in a list view, the system chooses which column set to use. This is based on:
The data type that is being displayed.
The system configuration, where a column set is specified on a method argument or quick find object data type.
When the column set is selected based on the data being displayed, the server works out the best column set to use by:
Looking for a column set for the first object returned.
Looking for a column set linked to the interfaces on the object.
Looking for a column set based on the object’s classification.
Create column sets
Column sets can be used to:
Support list views.
Manage related items support - to identify properties and relationships on relationships.
The following diagram illustrates the process of creating and implementing a new column set:
 Click a box in the diagram to go to the associated procedure.
Create a column set
You can create a new column set to define a custom view of an object when displayed in the list view window.
1. Click New > Administration > Column Set in the Desktop Client. The New Column Set form is displayed.
2. Type a Name and Description for the column set.
3. If you want to use the column set with the List Edit control, select the Column set has been enabled for list editing option. See List editing for more information about the List Edit control.
4. Click Finish.
Once the column set has been created, column items can be added to it.
Create column set items
You can create new column items to include custom properties for objects when displayed in the tree view.
To configure the column set items, right-click the column set, and click Manage Column Items on the shortcut menu. The Manage Column Items dialog box appears.
There are two tabs in the right pane, Library and Navigate Schema. You can create or add column items to this column set using either tab.
For more information about using the Library tab, see Create column set items using the Library tab (Modified in an update).
For more information about using the Navigate Schema tab, see Create column set items using the Schema Navigation tab (Modified in an update).
Create column set items using the Library tab
1. In the right or lower pane, select the Library tab, and click Create. The New Column Item form appears.
2. Type a Name and Description for the column set item.
3. The following properties are not needed for the list view, only for managing related items support.
Property
Description
Edge definition
The relationship or edge to be navigated. This is an optional property.
 Be sure to use the edge definition for all column items that use the same relationship definition path.
Property Edge Alias
Alias used for the relationship between the property definition and the edge definition. This property is used for reporting, and is
not required for regular column items.
Property definition UID
The UID of the property definition that the column item will display.
Dynamic item
Select this option when the column item is created from a relationship definition or property definition which is expanded from a
link interface.
Graph Edge name
The name of the edge definition the column item will display. This property is used for reporting, and is not required for regular
column items.
4. Click Finish to create the column set item. The column set item is added to the Library tab.
5. Select the new column item in the Library tab and click the 
 button (or the 
 button if the form is in landscape mode) to relate the new column item to the column set displayed in the left (or top) window.
 Some of the options on the form are mutually exclusive.
Create column set items using the Schema Navigation tab
The Navigate Schema tab of the Manage Column Items dialog box allows you to select one or more property definitions, relationship definitions, or edge definitions, create column items from these definitions, and then add the column
items to the column set.
Activate the Navigate Schema tab
In the right pane of the Manage Column Items dialog box (or the lower pane, if the form is in landscape mode), click the Navigate Schema tab.
If the column set is related to an interface definition or a class definition, the schema navigation tree appears when you click the Navigate Schema tab. If the column set is related to an interface definition, the schema navigation tree is
populated with that interface definition. If the column set is related to a class definition, the schema navigation tree is populated with that class definition, and the default expansion of the tree is to the class definition's realized interfaces.
If the column set is not related to an interface definition or a class definition, the Find Class Definition dialog box appears. Enter search criteria and then select a class definition from the list that appears. Once you have selected a class
definition, the schema navigation tree appears on the Navigate Schema tab. The schema navigation tree is populated with the selected class definition, and the default expansion of the tree is to the class definition's realized interfaces.
By default, the Navigate Schema tab displays the following nodes for the starting interface definition:
Exposed property definitions
Related edge definitions
Relationship definitions
To expand other nodes, right-click the interface definition, and select a command from the shortcut menu.
Click Details on the item's shortcut menu to view the details of the selected item.
Click the column headers to sort the items based on that column.
Select definitions for new column items
To create column items from items displayed on this tab, select one or more property definitions, edge definitions, or relationship definitions, and then click the 
 button.
 If the dialog box is in landscape mode (meaning that the Navigate Schema and Library panes are displayed below the column item pane), click the 
 button to create column items.
Edit column items before creation
Before the column items are created in the database, you can edit certain properties of the column items in the Manage Column Items dialog box.
You can edit the following properties for each column item in this dialog box.
Property
Description
Name
The name of the column item.
Description
The description of the column item.
Property definition UID
The UID of the property definition that the column item displays.
Edge definition
The name of the edge definition that the column item displays.
 Be sure to use the edge definition for all column items that use the same relationship definition path.
Dynamic item
This option is selected, or set to true, when the column item is created from a relationship definition or property definition which is
expanded from a link interface.
 This dialog box has some of the same features of the List Edit window, including:
sorting the list by clicking the column header.
filtering the list by using the Filter 
 button.
freezing a column by using the Freeze This Column command on a cell's shortcut menu.
exporting the contents of the dialog box to Excel by using the Export to Excel command on a cell's shortcut menu (for more information, see Export list to Excel).
Click OK to add the items to the column set.
Click OK on the Managed Items dialog box to save changes to the column set and add new column items to the database.
 Click the Remove a selected item 
 button to remove the relationship between a column item and a definition. The column item remains in the database.
Associate a column set with an interface
You must associate a new column set with an interface to use it. When you create a relationship between a column set and an interface definition, any object that has instantiated that interface can be displayed using that column set.
To associate a column set with an interface, find the interface definition (Find > Schema > Interface Definition), and then use a drag-and-drop operation to place the column set onto the interface definition.

Cumulative column sets on classified objects
In much the same way as classified objects can relate to forms at various levels of a classification tree, classified objects can also relate to a specific column set based on their classification in a classification tree. The difference with
column sets is that the potential column sets for the class must be related to an interface of the object, and then each column set (ISPFColumnSet) forms a second relation with the classification (ISPFObjectClass).
Take for example a classification structure for a custom class DEVTag:
Class
DEVTag
Primary Interface
IDEVTag
The goal for this example is to have different column sets for viewing Electrical equipment, Process equipment, and Process vessels, instead of a general column set for all DEVTags.
First, the column sets are created as in the Create Column Set section.
All the column sets are then related to the IDEVTag interface as potential column sets for DEVTag. Drag the column set onto the interface to form the List View Column Sets relationship.
The column sets now need to be related to the correct positions in classification tree using drag-and-drop operations to form the relationship classification items:
In the above example, DEVTags, created using the classification structure, will assume the following column sets:
Classification of DEVTag
Column set assumed
Reason
DEV Equipment
DEVTag
Column set directly related to
classification
DEV Process Equipment
DEVTag-Process
Column set directly related to
classification
DEV Process Vessel
DEVTag-Process-Vessel
Column set directly related to
classification
DEV-Process-equipment driver
DEVTag-Process
Column set inherited from next
highest parent set with a column
set
 If multiple column sets are related to an interface of a non-classified object, the software will display an error when a list view is requested; this will need to be resolved by an administrator removing relationships until there is only
one column set for the interface.
Overriding cumulative column sets for Quick Find
In the DEV Equipment example in the previous section, the created DEVTags all now carry different column set relationships. Although this is desired behavior for identifying properties on a specific classification, it means that if a generic
QFind is executed for all DEV tags, then the column set used for the display of all the returned tags is that of the first alphabetically retrieved object.
This behavior can be overridden in the QFind method by specifying a specific column set to be used for all tags as an argument in the Column set to present results box.
Manage column items (modified in an update)
The following applies if you are using a version of the software before Update 46. For the latest instructions, see Manage related items.
Column set items can be managed in the Desktop Client using the Manage Column Items dialog box.
Right-click the column set, and click Manage Column Items on the shortcut menu. The Manage Column Items dialog box is displayed.
See Manage related items for details on how to use the Library tab of this form to create and manage column items.
See Create column set items for details on how to use the Library tab and the Navigate Schema tab to create and manage column items.
Related Items (left or top) pane columns
Column
Description
Name
Column item name
Description
Description of column item
Display As
The column item display name
Size
Width of column
Order Value
Sequence in which the item appears.
Property
The property definition the column item displays.
Edge
The edge definition the column item displays.
 Be sure to use the edge definition for all column items that use the same relationship definition path.
Graph/Edge Alias
The alias used for the relationship between the property definition and the edge definition.
Dynamic
This property should be set to True when navigating to relationship properties or objects.
Configs
The configuration in which the column item is set to conditionally display when used in a dynamic form section.
Graph/Edge Name
Name of the edge definition the column item displays.
Display As, Size, and Order Value can be edited directly by clicking in the box.
Configuring automatic sorting using column sets (modified in an update)
The following applies if you are using a version of the software before Update 46. For the latest instructions, see Manage related items.
To configure automatic sorting, you need to find the column set related to the item you want to configure for automatic sorting in the list view and/or tree view in Desktop Client. Once you have found the column set, you must configure the
automatic sorting using the Manage Column Items dialog box in the Desktop Client, which allows you to configure multiple column items by which to sort items.
The two options used for configuring automatic sorting are:
Sort Sequence - determines the priority order of the column items to sort on.
Sort Order - determines whether to sort by ascending, descending, or none.
Example
This example shows how to configure design documents to automatically sort on revision and version in both the list view and tree view; first sorting on revision in ascending order, and then on version in descending order.
1. In the Desktop Client, find the design document column sets for the list view and tree view:
Click Find > Administration > Column Set, and type *SPFDoc*.
Verify the column sets returned by the find are the ones you want to modify:
Right-click the SPFDocumentVersion column set, and select Show List View Interface Definitions. This returns the interface definition ISPFDocumentVersion, so we know this is the correct column set to use for the
list view sorting.
Right-click the SPFDocumentVersionTreeDisplay column set, and select Show Tree View Display Class Definitions. This returns the class definition SPFDesignDocVersion, so we know this is the correct column set
to use for the tree view sorting.
2. Configure the list view column set for automatic sorting:
Right-click the SPFDocumentVersion column set, and select Manage Column Items.
To sort design documents on revision in ascending order, do the following:
Set the Sort Sequence property for the Revision column item to 1.
Set the Sort Order property to Ascending.
To sort design document revisions on version in descending order, do the following:
Set the Sort Sequence property for the Version column item to 2.
Set the Sort Order property to Descending.
Click OK to save changes.
3. Configure the tree view column set for automatic sorting:
Right-click the SPFDocumentVersionTreeDisplay column set, and select Manage Column Items.
To sort design documents on revision in ascending order, do the following:
Set the Sort Sequence property for the Revision column item to 1.
Set the Sort Order property to Ascending.
To sort design document revisions on version in descending order, do the following:
Set the Sort Sequence property for the Version column item to 2.
Set the Sort Order property to Descending.
Click OK to save changes
Automatic sorting in the list view window
1. Using the Quick Find menu, find all design documents. The find results in the list view window should be automatically sorted according to the configuration.
2. Right-click a document that has multiple versions, and click Revision Relationship > Show All Versions to expand all versions. The relationship expansion results in the list view window should be automatically sorted according to
the configuration.
3. Right-click a document that has multiple revisions, and click Master Relationship > Show All Revisions to expand all revisions. The relationship expansion results in the list view window should be automatically sorted according to
the configuration.
Automatic sorting in the Tree view
1. Expand the document category in the tree view to display a design document with multiple versions.
2. Right-click the category, and click Show Classified Item.
3. Right-click the classified item, and click Revision Relationship > Show All Versions to expand all versions. The versions should be automatically sorted according to the configuration.
4. Expand the document category in the tree view to display a design document with multiple revisions.
5. Right-click the category, and click Show Classified Item.
6. Right-click the classified item, and click Master Relationship > Show All Revisions to expand all revisions. The revisions should be automatically sorted according to the configuration.
 The New Items window will also automatically sort items according to the configuration.
If you define custom sorting for list views, users can edit that sorting using the Custom Sort dialog box in the list view. New list views that display items with automatic sorting default back to the administrator-defined sorting.
To change the custom sort order on a particular list view, right-click the list view column header, and click Sort > Custom Sort. Select the column that you want to change to be ascending or descending, and then click Ascending or
Descending to change the sort order. The software immediately applies the change to the list view window.
New items that are added to list view windows with automatic sorting using the shortcut menu or a drag-and-drop operation are added to the bottom of the list and the configured automatic sort is removed from the list view window.
Select Document category from the tree view. Hold down the Ctrl key, and drag Document category into the All Revisions list view.
Right-click the list view column header, and click Sort > Custom Sort. The software removes the configured automatic sorting.
Manage column items for edit (modified in an update)
The following applies if you are using a version of the software before Update 46. For the latest instructions, see Manage related items.
Column set items can be managed for use in dynamic create forms and the List Edit control using the Manage Column Items for Edit dialog box. The List Edit control is used to edit line lists and other lists of data. For more information
about the List Edit control, see List Edit window.
Right-click the column set, and click Manage Column Items for Edit on the shortcut menu. The Manage Column Items for Edit dialog box appears.
The following items appear on both the Manage Column Items dialog box and the Manage Column Items for Edit dialog box.
Related Items (left or top) pane columns
Column
Description
Name
Column item name
Description
Description of column item
Display As
The column item display name
Size
Width of column
Order Value
Sequence in which the item appears.
Property
The property definition the column item displays.
Edge
The edge definition the column item displays.
 Be sure to use the edge definition for all column items that use the same relationship definition path.
Graph/Edge Alias
The alias used for the relationship between the property definition and the edge definition.
Dynamic
This property should be set to True when navigating to relationship properties or objects.
Configs
The configuration in which the column item is set to conditionally display when used in a dynamic form section.
Graph/Edge Name
Name of the edge definition the column item displays.
Display As, Size, and Order Value can be edited directly by clicking in the box.
The following items appear only on the Manage Column Items for Edit dialog box.
Column
Description
Disable View
Hide this column when viewing data.
Hide on Dynamic Form
This property can be set to True to hide a column item from showing on any dynamic forms.
Disable Edit

Readonly

Allow Create

Max Value

You can use either the Library tab or the Navigate Schema tab to create and manage column items.
See Manage related items for details on how to use the Library tab of this form to create and manage column items.
See Create column set items using the Schema Navigation tab for details on how to use the Navigate Schema tab to create and manage column items.
You can also set the column items to use for a list in the List Management Console. Fore more information, see List Management Console and Configure column items for a list.
For more information on dynamic sections, see Dynamic sections.
Configure entry point default search filters
The following applies if you are using a version of the software before Update 46. For the latest instructions, see Manage related items.
You can configure the default API Entry Point object types, such as name and description, to display as filters when you select 
 FILTER on the Search menu in the Web Client. This allows you to control the search results returned using
the filters for each API Entry Point object type.
1. In the Desktop Client, locate the API Entry Point, and click Show Column Set > Manage Column Items.
2. Select the IsSearchDefault attribute for each object type you want to use as the search filter defaults in the Web Client. Any search includes the attributes by default in its criteria.
3. Click Administration > API Services > Regenerate Metadata to refresh the metadata.
 Only string-based properties can be used as the filter defaults.
Configure column items by configuration (modified in an update)
The following applies if you are using a version of the software before Update 46. For the latest instructions, see Manage related items.
You can configure property attribute column items in a column set to conditionally display in the same dynamic form section when selected from different configurations or plants. This functionality allows you to choose to display the
properties in a form based on the selected configuration.
 Do not use configuration top or the column items will not appear in the column set. This is due to a technical limitation.
To configure a property attribute using the Manage Column Items dialog box, click in the Configs column for the property, and choose the configuration from the list.
Manage related items
The Manage Related Items dialog box provides the user with an interface to display and maintain related items for a specified relationship. Examples are Manage Related Items for Form, Manage Related Items for Column Sets, and
Manage Menus.
The following diagram shows a typical Manage Related Items dialog box (for the SPFDBFile form, in this example).
1
Parent item (shown in title form)
2
Related Items pane (with configurable columns)
3
Library pane
The left pane represents a list of related items (in this example, form sections), the properties on the objects, and the relationships that can be edited directly in the list view window. These updates are saved to the database when you click
OK and ignored when you click Cancel.
The right pane displays a library that contains a set of valid items that can be related to the object (in the example in the diagram, form sections). The library allows the user to create new items, delete items, and relate items to the original
object.
The library control can be turned on or off via the method arguments. To relate an item, select it in the Library pane, and click the Relate Selected Item 
 button. The item selected will appear in the list on the left, and you can click each
cell to edit or add values.
You can change the positions of the two panes on this dialog box. Click the Switch Orientation 
 button to switch to portrait orientation. In portrait orientation, the Related Items pane appears at the top of the dialog box and the Library
pane appears at the bottom. In portrait orientation, click the Relate Selected Item 
 button on the left side of the Library pane to add items to the Related Items pane and relate them to the original object. Click the Switch Orientation
 button again to return to landscape orientation.
The following example shows the dialog box in portrait orientation.
Related Items (left or upper pane) buttons
Move selected item up.
Move selected item down.
Click to remove the selected item from the form. The relationship between the item and the definition is removed, but
the item remains in the database.
Library (right or lower pane) buttons
Create - Opens the dialog box for creating a new object. On completion, the object is immediately saved to the
database.
Update - Opens the Update dialog box for the item selected in the library list. On completion, the object is
immediately saved to the database.
Deletes the selected library item. On completion, the object is immediately removed from the database.
Find - Enter an item name (or part of the name plus wildcards*) in the Find box, and click Find. The results are
displayed in the Library pane.
Clear - Clears the Library pane.
Advanced filtering - Opens a dialog to enable property filtering on the library properties.
 or 
Relate item selected in the Library pane to the parent item. The related item will also be displayed in the Related
Items pane.
Dialog box manipulation buttons
Switch orientation - Changes the orientation of the Library pane and Related Items pane from landscape (the Related
Items pane is on the left, and the Library pane is on the right) to portrait (the Related Items pane is on the top of the
dialog box, and the Library pane is on the bottom).
For more information about the ManageRelatedItems API that is used by method to open these controls, see ManageRelatedItems API argument summary.
ManageRelatedItems API argument examples
This section describes typical usage and configuration of the methods for managing related items. To see the actual arguments configured, refer to the example methods in the delivered systems. To view these methods, query for methods
by client API = Manage Related Items and compare them all in one list view.
 You can use the Suppress Open in New Tab in Web Client option in a method to avoid launching actions that open into separate browser tabs.
Managing items that cannot exist without their parents
There is no library - as the related items cannot exist on their own and can only be related to one parent.
Users can edit the related items, as well as the relationship properties:
properties on the related item
properties (sequence) on the relationship
An example is the Manage Menu Items command (ManageMenuMenuItems)
Using the library to manage item relationships
This is the most common configuration of this method, where it is used to manage which objects are related and to configure properties on the relationships. Careful use of the column set presents an excellent tabulated view of all related
items and their relationship properties.
An example is Manage Access Groups from relationship definitions (ManageRelDefAccessGroups).
Using library to manage related objects as well as the relationships
The point here is that if you want to edit the related item properties and have, the updates edit the original object wherever it is used.
An example here is Manage Engineering Numbering System (ManageENS) where it is possible to change the properties on the ENS items, as well as the relationships to the ENS definition.
Some of the ENS items can be used on more than one ENS definition; you are protected from it inadvertently modifying the properties of the ENS item. When the dialog is initially displayed, these properties are locked and you must select
the unlock button before you can change these properties. If these properties should not be updated in the list view window, then they should not be included in the column set. The Update button would still be available.
Filtering the library
Manage methods on an interface (ManageInterfaceDefMethods) uses the following filter in Argument 7 to restrict the library to only available in desktop methods.
ISPFMethod~SPFAvailableinDesktop~=~TRUE
Making use of the Tree view in the library
This is used where there are many potential library items, and it is helpful to be able to navigate to other related objects.
An example of this is managing interfaces for a method (SysAdmUpdate).
Use the Manage Related Items form
For more information about using the Manage Related Items dialog box, see the following topics.
Create and relate a new item
Find an existing item
Add an existing item
Update an existing item
Delete (terminate) a relationship
Delete an item
Create and relate a new item
1. Click Create to display the New Item dialog box.
This will use the class definition specified (method arguments) to get the standard Create dialog box for this item.
2. Create the new item and click OK in the New Item dialog box.
The new item is displayed in the Library pane.
3. In the Library pane, select the item, and click Relate Selected Item 
.
The new item is related to the parent item and now appears in the Related Items pane.
4. Use 
 and 
 to change the order of the item.
5. Click OK.
Find an existing item
1. Enter the item name, or part of the name plus wildcards*, in the Find box (for example *Q*).
2. Click Find.
The results are displayed in the Library pane.
 Click Advanced to filter the search by comparing properties.
Add an existing item
1. Use Find to display the item in the Library pane.
2. Select the item, and click Relate Selected Item 
.
The new item is related to the parent item and also appears in the Related Items pane.
3. Use 
 and 
 to change the order of the item.
4. Click OK.
Update an existing item
 When an item is updated, it will be changed on all the dialog boxes on which it appears.
Items can be updated in several ways.
From the Library pane
1. Display the item in the Library pane, select it, and click Update.
2. Update the item, and click OK in the Update Item dialog box.
3. Click OK in the Manage Related Items dialog box.
From the Related Items pane
1. Select the item in the Related Items pane, and click Update.
2. Make the changes in the Related Items pane cells.
3. To save the changes, click OK in the Manage Related Items dialog box.
Delete (terminate) a relationship
Select the item in the Related Items pane, and click Remove Selected Item 
.
 Only the relationship between the item and the parent item is removed. This action will not delete the relationship from any other items that may be related. The item is removed from the list in the Related Items pane.
Delete an item
 This action deletes the actual item, not just the relationship.
Select the item in the Library pane, and click Delete. Click OK to confirm the operation.
 The delete will not succeed if the item is related to other items and therefore fails the schema rules.
Edit relationships
 This command is available depending upon an object's configuration.
1. In the Edit Relationships dialog box, do the following:
If there are alternative relationships to choose from, click the Relationship Name box, and use the down arrow to select the appropriate relationship from the list.
If a relationship supports attributes, you can edit the attributes by clicking the Maintain Attributes hyperlink for the relationship.
Select the Apply to similar items check box to specify whether the relationship selected in the Relationship Name column applies to all similar objects in the table.
2. Click OK to save the changes, and close the Edit Relationships dialog box.
View related items
The View Related Items command displays the Details dialog box for the selected object and one tab for each relationship you can navigate.
Right-click the object (for example, the IPD_Analyzer Template Version template document), and click View Related Items on the shortcut menu.
Configure forms
Here is a four-and-a-half minute video that shows you how to generate a form from a class definition and how to customize the form.
Forms are composed of three main components:
Form purpose
The form purpose controls the form section's structure. Each form purpose is displayed as a tab on the View Form dialog box.
Sections
Sections define a collection of display items (properties or relations). Sections may be either created or re-used from the database. Once a section is created, display items can be added to the section.
A section can be attached to multiple forms with any changes to that section automatically updated on all forms where it is included.
Display items
Display items are used to present data or relationships on a form. After a display item is created, the display item can then be added to a section for inclusion on a form. A display item can be a member of multiple sections. If a display
item is updated, the changes are automatically updated on the forms where it is included.
1
Purposes
2
Sections
3
Display items
Forms can be created in three different ways:
Generating a form automatically base on class definition and its realized interfaces.
Using the New Form dialog box to manually create the form.
Copying and editing an existing form.
 There can be minor differences in form sections and display items between forms created for the Desktop Client and those created for the Web Client. This is to help maximize performance in the Web Client, as well as to support
responsive design and form configuration.
A classified object can have alternative forms for different classifications of the class definition. The form is related to a class definition and used for all GUI operations of objects of that class definition. The SPFObjClassForm relationship
definition is used to navigate to the correct form.
Form section precedence
Because sections of a form can be related to roles, though access groups, to allow or prevent access to those sections and the display items associated with them, it is possible for a user with multiple roles selected to have access to
multiple sections with different configurations. By creating a hierarchical order, or precedence, for the sections, you can determine which section users see when they have multiple roles selected.
The different versions of a section are related using the SPFFormSectionsPrecedence relationship definition.
For more information, see Setting precedence for form sections.
Generate a form from a class definition
For more information about generating a form from a class definition see the following topics.
Form generation process
Default form generation
Generate form section for an interface
Form generation overrides
Form generation process
Forms can be generated from a shortcut menu command on a class definition. The basic generation process is as follows:
1. Find all the interface definitions on the class definition, via the realized relationships, and expand.
2. Create a form section for each interface definition found with a realizes order value of greater than 0 (unless overridden in code).
3. Create a display item for each property definition found with an exposes order value of greater than 0 (unless overridden in code).
4. Create a display item for each required relationship.
Default form generation
Forms can be generated via shortcut menu commands:
Generate form for class definition:
If a form already exists for the class definition, you are presented with a confirmation to overwrite it.
All existing display items and sections not referenced by other forms are deleted and recreated.
Generate form for schema component:
After a confirmation, this re-generates all forms for all class definitions linked to the schema component.
The following rules apply:
A common Name, Description, and General Information section is used on all objects. See Summary of Delivered Core GUI Model for details.
Administration forms include the ContainerID in the section with Name and Description.
Additional display items are added for configuration controlled classes to display the query and existing configuration of the object.
Sections and display items are generated as follows:
A section is generated for any interface that:
Has a realizes order value > -1 (so the model can control forms).
Contains properties or relationships that need display items (see below).
A display item is generated for:
Properties with an exposes order value > -1.
Required relationships: Check cardinality (Include End1 relationship definitions if the min2 = 1 or max2 = 1. Include End2 relationship definitions if min1 = 1).
Abstract relationships are not included.
Relationship definitions on link interfaces are always included, when they are found.
An additional section is generated with display items for optional interfaces that:
Have a realizes order value of > -1 and have no properties or relationships.
Generate form section for an interface
Form sections for an interface can be generated via a shortcut menu command on the interface definition to make it easy to do partial form updates:
The sections are generated using the same rules described in Default form generation.
It does not allow the regeneration of core delivered interfaces (see the Summary of Delivered Core GUI Model section for a list of these interfaces).
It offers a default ContainerID based on the class definition.
The resultant sections can then be used on forms for class definitions that realize that interface.
Regeneration of an existing section does not delete the section or its relationships to any forms; it just updates the section and its display items, for example, order value and new display items. (It does not change the Display As
value because this may have been changed on the form already.)
Form generation overrides
A number of form generation overrides are set in the code. When a form is generated from a class definition, these overrides will prevent certain sections from being included on the form. (As opposed to just honoring the realizes and
exposes order value of -1 to hide it.)
If, when a form is generated, one or more of the sections do not display, and they are not hidden, they may be overridden by the code.
 It is possible to display the override properties on a form by manually creating a new section and relating to the form.
These include:
Interface definition overrides
Property definition overrides
Relationship definition overrides
Section display as overrides
Form section order overrides
Display item order overrides
Example
In the following example, the IsInterfaceIncluded override prevents the section for the ISPFObjectWorkflowStepChkLst interface from appearing on the form for the SPFWorkflowStepChkLst class definition.
A full list of delivered form overrides is given in Delivered form generation overrides.
Create a form manually
1. Click File > New > Administration > Form in the Desktop Client. The New Form dialog box is displayed.
2. Type a name and description for the form.
3. Click Finish.
4. Relate the new form to a class definition using a drag-and-drop operation.
If you want the form to be used for a specific classification, you must relate the form to the required classification using a drag-and-drop operation, as well as relating it to the class definition.
Relating the form to a class definition does not automatically create sections or display items. These must be added to the form manually.
5. Manually add the required sections and display items to the new form. See Create form sections and Create new display items.
Copy a form
Copying a form allows you to create a new form definition that references the same sections and display items as the original form. It also copies all the associated relationships.
1. Select the form to be copied.
 The copy can be edited if it does not match exactly.
2. Right-click the form, and click Copy on the shortcut menu. The Copy Form form is displayed.
3. Type a name and description for the form.
4. Click Finish.
 When the form is copied, the relationship to the class definition is also copied, but the original form's sections and display items are not copied.
5. Add or create the form sections and display items.
Form builder
The Form Builder is a convenient way to display the form structure and access the Manage Sections and Manage Display Items forms.
To display the Form Builder:
1. Right-click a form, and click Form Builder on the shortcut menu.
2. When generating a form from a class definition, click Yes when asked if you wish to edit the form.
The Form Builder is displayed in the list view window.
3. To configure the form sections, right-click the form in the Form Builder, and click Manage Sections on the shortcut menu.
1
Section
2
Display item
4. To configure the form display items, right-click the section in the Form Builder, and click Manage Display Items on the shortcut menu.
You can drag sections from a form on the Desktop Client onto a form displayed in the Form Builder.
You can drag display items from a form on the Desktop Client onto a form displayed in the Form Builder.
You can drag display items between sections in the Form Builder.
You can open two Form Builder windows and drag sections and display items between the forms.
Create form sections
1. Right-click the form object, and click Manage Sections on the shortcut menu. The Manage Related Items for Forms dialog box is displayed (see below for details).
2. In the right pane, click Create. The New Item dialog box is displayed.
3. Type a Name and Description for the section.
 If you are creating a new section for use on an update form, do not include the text "RevScheme" in the name of the section. If this text is included in the name, the system treats it as an overridden section element and
retrieves the next or initial major and minor values. This can result in corrupted data.
4. In the Display As box, type the section name as it should appear on the form.
5. Provide values in the remaining boxes using the information below, and then click Finish to create the new section.
Property
Description
Is section visible
Select to make the section visible in the form.
Form purposes
The type of form tab on which the section will be displayed. There can be multiple sections on the same tab that can be associated
with more than one purpose so they can appear on different versions of the same form. See the table below for options.
Property level access control
Overrides the display items setting. Options are:
Read only - All display items in the section are read-only regardless of their individual settings.
Read/Write - All display items in the section are editable regardless of their individual settings.
Collapse behavior
Determines whether the form displays the section in collapsed or expanded mode. The options are:
Auto Initially Collapsed
Auto Initially Expanded
Initially Collapsed
Initially Expanded
Dynamic section driven from column set
Select to make the section dynamic in the form.
(See Dynamic sections)
Indicate what level the section is in the complex structure.
Determines which object of a complex object to set the properties on.
If specified section will be presented in a tab on the form using the name or #nnnn string.
String in which you can enter a name for the tab.
Once the section has been created, the display items can be added to it. See Create new display items.
Form Purpose options
Purpose
Description
Activate
Activates a reserved document. This option is different from create because some properties set during document reservation can be
read-only during activate. An example of a read-only property is Name.
Copy
Section is to be displayed on copy forms. When an object is copied, only those display items that are associated with a section that has
the Copy option selected are copied.
Create
Section is to be displayed on create and copy forms.
Get info
Get Information. Section is to be displayed on Properties or Details dialog boxes. All display items are presented as read-only so the
same section can be used for Update and Get information.
HisRev
Section is to be displayed on history revision forms.
Query
Section is to be displayed on query forms.
Reclassify
Section is to be displayed on reclassify forms.
Revise
Section is to be displayed on revise forms.
Sign Off
Section is to be displayed only on sign off forms.
Update
Section is to be displayed only on update forms.
AttachFile
Section is to be displayed only on attach forms.
Upload
Section to be displayed on upload forms.
Hidden sections
You may want the form to have display items that are not shown to the user. This is achieved by not displaying the section on the form (hidden sections). To do this, leave the Is section visible check box unselected.
Dependant pick lists
The Indicate what level the section is in the complex structure is used to create a dependency between related items in complex objects to control what is actually displayed.
Example
In the above example, the tag has a relationship to both Unit and Area, but there is also a relationship between Unit and Area. When you pick a unit (for example 01), the SPFDisplayItemDependentItem relationship definition restricts the
pick list to 01 areas (Area 01A, and Area 01B).
Dynamic sections
You may want to define a base form and have an additional section dynamically generated from the column items configured for the item's list view. To do this, select Dynamic section driven from column set option.
For a given form purpose, a form can have only one section defined as being a dynamic one, unless they have different complex levels. Display items are dynamically generated in the same order as column items, sorted by order value.
The default create values of these display items will respect the SPFCellDefaultCreateValue property in ISPFColumnSetColumnItem interface definition, along with the Mandatory and DisplayAs properties. They should also respect the
ReadOnly property, when the form purpose is Reclassify, Revise, SignOff, Update, or Activate.
No display item will be generated for the following column items:
Property definition, edge definition, or relationship definition of a column item, if it is related to a display item already on the form.
Exposed property definitions of IObject.
Dynamic item property set to True.
Edge definition UID is set and property definition UID is not Name.
Edge definition column item with multiple relationship definitions in path definition.
In addition to selecting the Dynamic section driven from column set option, the appropriate complex level must be set. Sections must be related to the form and form purpose. The dynamic sections are then added to the form.
You can hide an attribute column item from any dynamic form by selecting Manage Column Items for Edit on the specific column set and setting the Hide on Dynamic Form property to True.
You can configure a property attribute column item to conditionally display in the same dynamic form section when selected from different configurations by selecting Manage Column Items and setting the property attribute Configs
option to the required configuration.
Control dynamic sections using SmartPlant Attribution Manager
You can edit the attribute content of column sets related to classifications using SmartPlant Attribution Manager. This controls how each form displays items on dynamic sections and dynamic section tabs.
If you want a form to display the contents of dynamic sections configured using SmartPlant Attribution Manager, set the Allow Attribution Manager Content form details property to True in the Desktop Client.
By default, the Allow Attribution Manager Content property is set to False, so that any existing forms are not affected by any changes made in SmartPlant Attribution Manager. You must set the property to True on each form that you
want to edit using SmartPlant Attribution Manager.
For more information, see Modify attributes.
Dynamic section tabs
You can create dynamic sections to be displayed as a set of tabs on a new form, each containing a set of column display items. In large complex forms, there can be many column items configured to display as a single list of display items,
which can be very long to scroll. Grouping column items by section tabs reduces the form length and groups specific items into manageable groups, such as when you create a new object. A form can be defined to contain multiple section
tabs, each displaying a logically grouped list of column items.
 This feature only applies to classified items.
When you select a classification to create a new object, (such as Create Tag), the New Create Tag form displays the dynamic sections as tabs, each containing an ordered group of column items, as shown in the following image:
 The tabs support all types of forms, such as update, query, details, and so on.
Create a section tab
To create a dynamic section tab, use the following steps:
1. Click Find > Administration > Form to display a list of forms in the system.
2. Right-click any form in the list, and click Manage Sections.
3. On the Manage sections toolbar, click Create to create a new form section.
4. Enter the details in the Name and Display as boxes. For example, Properties.
5. In Attribution Manager Dynamic Section details, click the Is Attribution Manager Section? option to display the dynamic section as a tab in a selected form.
6. Click Finish.
You do not need to relate the new section to the selected form as it is dynamically applied to the form when required.
Form sections given a form purpose display only on forms configured for that form purpose. For example, form sections given the form purpose GetInfo displays only on the Details form.
You can also make these section tabs using the Loader.
You can also create a dynamic section tab using SmartPlant Attribution Manager. For more information, see Modify attributes.
After creating the section tabs:
1. Configure a column set dedicated to a classification and add the column items you want displayed. For more information, see Configure column sets.
2. In the Tree view, relate the classification to the column set by dragging and dropping. This uses the SPFColumnSetObjClass relationship definition to make the relationship.
3. In the Tree view, right-click the column set, and click, Show Column Items.
4. Right-click a column item, and click Update.
5. In the Dynamic form section box, select the form section previously created, such as Properties. The column item will now display on the Properties tab.
 You can also set the column items for a dynamic section tab using SmartPlant Attribution Manager. For more information, see Modify the attribute sequence.
6. Click Finish.
If a column item is not related to a section tab, it is shown in a list on the Main Details tab, along with any default column items, such as Name and Description.
The column set removes any gaps on a new hard-configured form and prevents any column item duplication.
Each column item in a section tab is displayed in the same order as the selected OrderValue when you select Manage Column Items for a column set. You can override this column order sequence using the Use Form Sequence
Override system option to prevent sequencing many column sets individually. For more information, see Configure column sets.
Picture mask types
Picture mask types are formats for display item data. The picture property on a display item is used for the masking in all cases, except for those properties that are date formats.
Validate dates
Picture properties can be used for validation rather than forcing formatting. This allows administrators to prevent users from inserting dates that are invalid, such as dates in the past.
Below are the constants that are supported and the logic performed. For example, if you enter FUTUREONLY in the picture, and the user enters a date that is before today, an error message appears.
Case "TODAY"
If ldatLocalDate.Date <> System.DateTime.Today Then
lstrReturnError = SPF.Client.Resources.SPFResources.GetStringWithParameters(9302, New String() {Picture})
End If
Case "FUTUREONLY"
If ldatLocalDate.Date <= System.DateTime.Today Then
lstrReturnError = SPF.Client.Resources.SPFResources.GetStringWithParameters(9302, New String() {Picture})
End If
Case "PASTONLY"
If ldatLocalDate.Date >= System.DateTime.Today Then
lstrReturnError = SPF.Client.Resources.SPFResources.GetStringWithParameters(9302, New String() {Picture})
End If
Case "NOTFUTURE" ' = today and past
If ldatLocalDate.Date > System.DateTime.Today Then
lstrReturnError = SPF.Client.Resources.SPFResources.GetStringWithParameters(9302, New String() {Picture})
End If
Case "NOTPAST" ' = today and future
Set date and time formats
Picture masks use format strings or characters to format the input data. Format strings can be combined to format the date and time. The date strings must be prefixed with DATE. For example:
To display the date and time as 06/01/2001 12:00 PM, you would enter DATEdd'/'MM'/'yyyy hh':'mm tt.
To display string literals that contain date and time separators or format strings, you must enclose the substring in single quotation marks ('). To display the date as June 06 at 3:00 PM, you would enter DATEMMMM dd 'at' h:mm tt.
 If the 'at' substring is not separated with single quotation marks, as shown, the result is June 06 aP 3:00PM. This is because the character t is read as the one letter AM/PM abbreviation in the input format string, as shown in
the following tables.
You can construct picture masks using the format strings and characters:
Format string description
This table lists all the valid format strings:
Format string
Description
Null String
No mask. The display item acts like a standard text box. This setting is the default.
d
One or two digit day.
dd
Two-digit day. Single digit values are preceded by a zero.
ddd
Three-character day of the week abbreviation.
dddd
Full day of the week name.
h
One or two digit hour using the 12-hour format.
hh
Two-digit hour using the 12-hour format. Single digit values are preceded by a zero.
H
One or two digit hour using the 24-hour format.
HH
Two-digit hour using the 24-hour format. Single digit values are preceded by a zero.
m
One or two digit minute.
mm
Two-digit minute. Single digit values are preceded by a zero.
M
One or two digit month number.
MM
Two-digit month number. Single digit values are preceded by a zero.
MMM
Three-character month abbreviation.
MMMM
Full month name.
s
One or two digit seconds.
ss
Two digit seconds. Single digit values are preceded by a zero.
t
One letter AM/PM abbreviation (AM is displayed as A).
tt
Two letter AM/PM abbreviation (AM is displayed as AM).
y
One digit year (2001 is displayed as 1).
yy
Last two digits of the year (2001 is displayed as 01).
yyyy
Full year (2001 is displayed as 2001).
Character description
This table lists all the characters that the input mask can contain:
Character
Description
U
Forces the entry to uppercase.
#
Digit placeholder.
.
Decimal placeholder. The actual character used is the one specified as the decimal placeholder in your international settings.
This character is treated as a literal for masking purposes.
,
Thousands separator. The actual character used is the one specified as the thousands separator in your international settings.
This character is treated as a literal for masking purposes.
A
Alphanumeric character placeholder (entry required). For example: a – z, A – Z, or 0 – 9.
a
Alphanumeric character placeholder (entry optional).
9
Digit placeholder (entry optional). For example: 0 – 9.
Literal
All other symbols are displayed as literal.
Use regular expressions
The regular expression processor in Microsoft .NET is used to process picture masks with character or digit placeholders. To use these placeholders, you must prefix the picture mask with the word "new".
The software validates two formats of expressions for the NEW (VB.NET) format and the previous format. We identify using the new format by prefixing the picture with NEW. For the old format, we convert it into the new format and then
evaluate it.
The following is replaced:
lstrRegExp = pstrPicture.Replace("#", "[0-9]")
lstrRegExp = lstrRegExp.Replace("?", ".")
lstrRegExp = lstrRegExp.Replace("*", ".*")
lstrRegExp = lstrRegExp.Replace("[!", "[^")
lstrRegExp = "^" & lstrRegExp & "$"
The following table lists some examples of valid picture masks.
Picture mask
Description
new^[A-z][0-9]?$
Input must be 1 alphabetical character plus an optional single numeric character.
new^[A-z]$
Input must be alphabetical characters only.
new^[A-z]{3}$
Input must be exactly 3 alphabetical characters.
The following table lists uses and examples for the character placeholders used in regular expressions.
Character
Description
Example
Results
^
The pattern must appear at the beginning of a string.
^abc
abc, abcdefg, abc123, ...
$
The pattern must appear at the end of a string.
abc$
abc, endsinabc, 123abc, ...
.
Matches any character.
a.c
abc, aac, acc, adc, aec, ...
|
Alternation. One of the alternatives must match.
bill|ted
ted, bill
{...}
Explicit quantifier notation.
ab{2}c
abbc
[...]
Explicit set of characters to match.
a[bB]c
abc, aBc
(...)
Logical grouping of part of an expression.
(abc){2}
abcabc
[^]
Negates a bracket expression. Matches one of any characters
EXCEPT those enclosed.
1[^02]
matches 13 but not 10 or 12
[-]
Range. Matches any characters within the range.
[1-9]
matches any single digit EXCEPT 0
*
0 or more of previous expression.
ab*c
ac, abc, abbc, abbbc, ...
+
1 or more of previous expression.
ab+c
abc, abbc, abbbc, ...
?
0 or 1 of previous expression; also forces minimal matching when an
expression might match several strings within a search string.
ab?c
ac, abc
\
Preceding one of the above, it makes it a literal instead of a special
character. Preceding a special matching character, see below.
a\sc
a c
For more information on regular expressions, please refer to the documentation delivered with Microsoft .NET.
Password mask on forms in the Web Client
You can use the picture properties to mask the password that is entered on forms in the Web Client. To do this, you must:
1. In the Desktop Client, click Find > Schema > Property Definition to search for the property definition you want to modify.
2. Right-click the property definition, and click Show Display Items.
3. Right-click the display item name, and click Update to modify the display item.
4. In the Update Display Item dialog box, type PASSWORD in the Property picture box. It supports lowercase format, too.
 This setting will mask the password in all the forms that have the display item. For more information on display items, see Display item types.
5. Click Finish to update the display item.
Manage form sections
A form's sections can be managed in the Desktop Client through the Manage Related Items dialog box. This dialog box can be used to create, update and re-order sections, terminate relationships, and delete sections.
To manage a form's sections
Right-click the form object, and click Manage Sections on the shortcut menu. The Manage Related Items dialog box is displayed.
See Manage related items and Create form sections for further details on how to use this form.
Create new section
1. In Manage Related Items dialog box, click Create to display the New Item form.
2. In the New Item form, create the new section, and click OK. The new section is displayed in the Library pane. See Create form sections for details.
3. Select the section, and click Relate Selected Item 
. The new section is related to the form and appears in the Related Items pane.
 If the dialog box is in portrait orientation, the Library pane appears at the bottom of the dialog box instead of on the right side of the dialog box. Click Relate Selected Item 
 in the Library pane to relate the new section to
the form.
4. Select the section, and click 
 and 
 to move the section up or down.
5. Click OK to save the changes.
Find an existing section
1. In the Manage Related Item for Forms dialog box, type a section name, or part of the name plus wildcards*, in the Find box (for example, *Process*).
2. Click Find.
The results are displayed in the Library pane.
Add an existing section
1. In the Manage Related Item for Forms dialog box, use the Find feature to display the required section in the Library pane.
2. Select the section, and click Relate Selected Item 
. The section is related to the form and appears in the Related Item pane.
 If the dialog box is in portrait orientation, the Library pane appears at the bottom of the dialog box instead of on the right side of the dialog box. Click Relate Selected Item 
 in the Library pane to relate the section to the
form.
3. Select the section, and click 
 and 
 to move the section up or down.
4. Click OK to save the changes.
Update an existing section
When a section is updated, it will be changed on all the forms on which it appears.
Sections can be updated in two different ways.
From the Library pane
1. In the Manage Related Item for Forms dialog box, find the required section in the Library pane, select the section, and click Update. The Update Section form is displayed.
2. Update the section, and click OK.
3. Click OK in the Manage Related Items dialog box.
From the Related Items pane
1. In the Manage Related Item for Forms dialog box, select the section in the Related Items pane, and click Update. The Update Section form is displayed.
2. Update the section, and click OK.
3. Click OK in the Manage Related Items dialog box.
Delete a section from a form
To delete a section from a form, it is only necessary to delete the relationship. The section will remain in the library for use by other forms.
In the Related Items pane, select the section, and click Remove a selected item 
. The section is deleted from the form.
 When the section is deleted, only the relationship between the section and the form is removed. This action will not delete the section from any other forms on which it appears.
Delete a section
 This action deletes the section from the database.
Display the item in the Library pane, and click Delete. A dialog box will request confirmation; click OK.
 The system will not delete the section if it is related to other forms and therefore fails the schema rules.
Manage form section access control (modified in an update)
The following applies if you are using a version of the software before Update 46. For the latest instructions, see Manage related items.
You can control the access to sections in the Desktop Client by choosing the type of access control you want to use on the form, either using access groups or configuring interface owning groups. Using access groups allows you to set the
type of access control at the form level and set the relationship to specific access groups at the section level. This means that you can restrict a section to a particular access group on one form, and on other forms the same section may
not be restricted at all, or may be restricted by an object interface relationship rather than by access group.
To set the type of control access required for a section on a form, select one of three property options in the Type of access control display item when you update an existing form or create a new one.
None (blank) - The default option. Allows you to display all sections on a form without any access control, so each section is visible to all access groups.
Access Group - Allows you to control access directly on a section and gives you a level of control that allows you to make a section hidden, visible (read-only), or editable when you relate an access group to a section. For more
information, see Configure section access groups (Modified in an update).
Interface - Allows you to create or continue to use owning groups using an object interface to control the access to sections on a form. For more information on setting up access owning group control using an object interface relationship,
see Configure section owning groups (Modified in an update).
 Using the interface definition owning group to control access impacts performance for operations, such as using shortcut menus, building forms, filling the properties grid, evaluating methods on shortcut menus, and others.
The interface definition owning group access control is disabled by default to help improve performance. For more information on enabling this functionality, see Interface owning group access control.
Configure section access groups (modified in an update)
The following applies if you are using a version of the software before Update 46. For the latest instructions, see Manage related items.
Setting the access control type to Access Group on the form allows you to quickly assign access groups to each section.
From the form section
1. Click Find > Administration > Form Section.
2. In the Find Form Section dialog box, enter the name of the form section, or use a wildcard to refine the name.
3. Right-click the section, and click Manage Access Groups.
4. In the Manage Related Items dialog for managing access groups, select each access group to have access to the section, and click Relate Selected Item 
.
If you want the section to be uneditable (read-only) by the selected access group, change the Readonly? option to True on each access group.
Access groups not related to the section cannot see the section on the form.
5. Click OK to save the changes.
From the Form Builder
1. Right-click a form, and click Form Builder.
2. Expand the form to display each of the form sections.
3. Right-click the section to which you want to add access control, and click Manage Access Groups.
4. In the Manage Related Items dialog for managing access groups, select each access group to have access to the section, and click Relate Selected Item 
.
 Change the Readonly? option to True on each access group, if you want the section to be uneditable (read-only) by the selected access group.
5. Click OK to save the changes.
The section is hidden to any access group that is not related to a section on the form.
If no access control is configured for a section, the section is visible for all users.
If you change the access groups for a section, it changes the access group on every form the section appears.
If you want to change the access groups for a specific section on a new form, but want to keep the access groups the same where ever the section appears on existing forms, you must make a copy of the section and add that to the
new form. You can then change the access groups related to the section copy without altering the access groups in the original section.
Configure section owning groups (modified in an update)
The following applies if you are using a version of the software before Update 46. For the latest instructions, see Manage related items.
Setting the access control to Interface allows you to configure owning groups to control access to parts of an object's data relating the owning group to the Interface containing the data. This prevents other owning groups from accessing
the data.
For more information on setting up interface access owning group control, see Interface owning group access control.
 If you have previously configured your system to use owning group access control on objects using the ISPFInterfaceDefDataAccessGroup interface, you must set the access property to Interface on every affected form to
continue to use that type of access control.
 You can search for all the forms that have been configured to use owning groups by running the Forms with Owning Group Control report.
Setting precedence for form sections (modified in an update)
The following applies if you are using a version of the software before Update 46. For the latest instructions, see Manage related items.
To prevent users with multiple roles selected from seeing duplicate or contradicting sections on a form, use the following steps to create a precedence for sections that are configured differently for different roles.
Drag the section with the lower precedence onto the section with the higher precedence.
 You can create a chain of sections using this relationship, and the highest precedence section available to a user, based on their selected roles, will be used to display the form.
Configure form title
You can configure the form title for Update, Copy, and View forms. The form title contains two parts:
The first part is not configurable, it shows the display name of the method associated with the form.
The second part is configurable, it includes any property of the form.
To configure the second part, you need to configure the Title parameter for the CreateObj, EditObj, and ShowObj client APIs or for any method associated to these client APIs. For more information, see Common methods.
The Title parameter can include any of the following:
Static text such as Testing and so on.
Constants such as $Plant, and so on.
Expressions such as obj.classdefobj.name, obj.name, and so on.
 These expressions must be enclosed within a set of ampersand (&) symbols, such as &obj.name&.
The Title parameter configured for any method associated to the CreateObj API can include only static text.
