This process step is used in the sample workflow, CDWGenerateGraphicsMapFile, delivered through Smart Community <https://smartcommunity.hexagonppm.com> . For more information, see Find sample data on Smart Community.
Process class name: SDVCDWGenerateGraphicsMapFile
Syntax: SDVCDWGenerateGraphicsMapFile, Arg1, Arg2, Arg3
Where:
Arg1: Specifies the relationship definition UID that is used to get the drawing representations related to the document revision object, which is attached to the workflow.
Arg2: Specifies the relationship definition UID that will be used to get the CDW objects or FDW tags related to the drawing representations extracted using Arg1.
Arg3: Optional argument. Specifies the graph definition UID that can be used as a pre-expansion of objects. This graph definition can be configured to traverse from document revisions, to drawing representations, to CDW objects, or to
FDW tags.
Example 1: Data transfer from CDW source system to CDW target system
SDVCDWGenerateGraphicsMapFile,SPFDesignDocRevisionDrawingRepresentation,SPFFDWDrawingRepresentationCDWObject
Example 2: Data transfer from CDW source system to FDW target system
SDVCDWGenerateGraphicsMapFile,FDWDocumentRevisionDrawingRepresentation,FDWDrawingRepresentationFDWTag
Configure a load job for exchanging documents and data automatically
This process step is used in the sample workflow, Auto-correlateWithExchangeData. It can be used in any of the publish workflows.
This process step is delivered through Smart Community <https://smartcommunity.hexagonppm.com> . For more information, see Find sample data on Smart Community.
Process class name: RunSPFExchangeData
Syntax: RunSPFExchangeData, Arg1
Where:
Arg1: Specifies the user-defined method name configured on the SPFExchangeData client API. This method is used for triggering the Exchange Data functionality. For more information, see Create a user-defined method for exchanging
data.
Example: RunSPFExchangeData, ExchangeCDWItemsFromPublishedDocument
ExchangeCDWItemsFromPublishedDocument is a sample user-defined method delivered through Smart Community, configured for exchanging CDW items related to a published document, when data is transferred from a CDW source
system to an FDW or HxGN SDx target system.
Step definition configuration
Workflow steps only exist in the context of a workflow template. Every step must be related to a step definition, which is used to describe the behavior of the step, and is selected from a library of step definitions. The following information is
specified on the step definition:
Step sequence
Step status
An accept route for all steps
A rejection route for validation or voting steps
The recipients of each step (can be users or roles)
The step duration (used to set the target date)
Checklists (for work assignment steps)
Create a new step definition
Custom step definitions can be an integral part of a custom workflow template. However, before a custom step definition can be used in an existing or custom workflow template, it must be defined. For more information about configuring
custom workflow templates, see Creating a new workflow template.
1. Click File > New > Workflow > Step definition to open the New Step Definition dialog box.
2. Type a unique name and description for the step definition.
3. In the Step class box, select the step class that the definition is related to.
4. In the Post class name box, type the post class name.
The post class name is the name of the process class to be run after the step related to this definition completes.
The post class name format is:
<Process class name>.<class>.<method>,parameter1,parameter2, and so on
where the number of parameters is determined by the method.
Example: CREATECHKLISTFORSTEP,XmtlDocs,True
The post class name provides a hook for customization, which can be used to perform additional processing after the step has completed and before the next step starts. For example, there is a post class name to generate a
checklist based on objects related to the main object in the workflow, and this is used on a transmittal issue step to create a checklist item for each document on the transmittal.
5. Click Finish to create the new step definition.
Set precedences for the workflow steps
To prevent a user from being assigned multiple steps in a workflow, steps can be ordered by precedence so that if a user receives a second concurrent step, the step with the lower precedence will be replaced by the step with the higher
precedence. To set the precedence between workflow steps, create an SPFStepDefPrecedence relationship definition between each step, as follows:
Drag the workflow step definition with the lower precedence onto the workflow step definition with the higher precedence.
 The group assignment control setting in the Update Step Recipients dialog box, in the Desktop Client, overrides the precedence set for the workflow step. If OneUserPerRole or OneUserPerStep group assignment control is
configured on the workflow step and that step is assigned to multiple users, you must claim the step to apply the precedence.
Understand workflow step precedences
The following table lists the default behavior when a user would be assigned two parallel workflow steps.
For example, in the internal technical query create form, when the technical query is issued:
If the same user is set to Technical query receipt notification and Respond to TQ steps, then the Technical query receipt notification step is skipped, and the Respond to TQ step is assigned to the user.
If a user role is set to Respond to TQ step and one of the users in that role is set to Technical query receipt notification step, then the Technical query receipt notification and the Respond to TQ steps are assigned to the same user. If
that user then claims the Respond to TQ step, the Technical query receipt notification step is terminated.
Workflow step 1
Workflow step 2
Outcome
Receipt notification
Initial review
Step 1 is terminated, and step 2 is assigned.
Initial review
Receipt notification
Step 2 is skipped or terminated.
Perform response
Consolidate responses
Step 1 is terminated, and step 2 is assigned.
Consolidate responses
Perform response
Step 2 is skipped or terminated.
Technical query receipt notification
Respond to TQ
Step1 is terminated, and step 2 is assigned.
Respond to TQ
Technical query receipt notification
Step 2 is skipped or terminated.
Action receipt notification
Perform action and respond
Step 1 is terminated, and step 2 is assigned.
Perform action and respond
Action receipt notification
Step 2 is skipped or terminated.
Perform initial review
Approve the review
Step 1 is terminated, and step 2 is assigned.
Approve the review
Perform initial review
Step 2 is skipped or terminated.
Transmittal receipt notification
Transmittal issued
Step 1 is terminated, and step 2 is assigned.
Transmittal issued
Transmittal receipt notification
Step 2 is skipped or terminated.
Step status creation
A workflow status is displayed on an object in a workflow to indicate how it is progressing through that workflow. The status is set on the object in the workflow at the completion of a step. This is configured on the workflow administration
form.
Workflow Status
Description
!WF-C
Workflows that are in progress
WF
In workflow
WF-A
Approved in workflow
WF-C
Workflow completed
WF-I
Issued in workflow
WF-R
Rejected in workflow
WF-V
Validated in workflow
Workflow status naming conventions
The status name appears in the list views of the workflow and the object. The following naming convention should be used.
Prefix with WF. 
Keep the name short. (For example, WF-D.)
Type a description.
Create a step status
A customized step status is a good way of showing users, at a glance, the status of an object in a workflow. Custom step status can be used in existing or custom workflow templates. For more information about configuring a custom
workflow template, see Creating a new workflow template.
1. Select File > New > Workflow > Step Status in the Desktop Client to display the New Workflow Step Status dialog box.
2. Type a unique name and description for the step status.
 You can view the workflow completion status details in the To Do List workflow step details grid by giving a status percentage for each workflow status in the New Workflow Step Status dialog box.
3. To give a workflow status percentage, type any range (1-100) in the Status percentage box in the Workflow status details section.
 You can view the workflow percentages in the Accept Percentage and Reject Percentage lists in the Create Workflow Template or Update Workflow Template dialog boxes.
4. Click Apply, and then click Finish.
The new status is now displayed in the status accept and reject status lists in each individual step of a workflow and in the system options, as well as in the boxes in the Create Workflow Template or Update Workflow Template forms.
For more information, see Set step status options.
Set step status options
The initial workflow status and the workflow completion status are set in the system options. To set up the workflow start and completion statuses for the workflow:
1. In the system options, scroll down to the Workflow options details section. For more information on the system options, see Configure the system options.
2. In the Initial workflow status box, select the status that will be first set when an object is attached to the workflow.
3. In the Completion workflow status box, select the status that will be set when the workflow is completed.
4. Select a column set in the Workflow viewer column set box to determine the workflow properties to be displayed in the Workflow step details window in the Web Client.
 You can either select a workflow column set from an existing column sets or create a new one. For more information on how to create and configure column sets, see Configure column sets.
Step checklist configuration
Each step in a workflow can have a checklist attached to it to be completed by the recipients.
Completion of these checklists can be mandatory as indicated by a * or optional.
The checklist items are presented in the To Do List against the step.
Checklists are defined with a name and description.
Each item in the check list is defined as a simple text string.
Create and manage a new checklist
If you want to associate a checklist to a workflow step, you must first create the checklist and its checklist items. Once created, the checklist can be set for any workflow step. For more information about configuring a custom workflow
template, see Creating a new workflow template.
1. Click File > New > Workflow > Step Checklist in the Desktop Client.
2. Type a new name for the checklist, and click Finish. The new check list is displayed in the Desktop Client.
3. In the Desktop Client, right-click the check list, and click Manage Checklist Items in the shortcut menu.
4. Click 
 (Create new check list item and relate it). The Create New Check List Item dialog box is displayed.
 You can also create a new item directly from the shortcut menu; see below.
5. Type a name and a description for the item.
6. Click OK. The new item is displayed in the Manage Checklist Items form.
7. Click OK.
 Click 
 Remove Selected Item to remove the relationship between a checklist item and a checklist. The item is deleted from the database.
Create new checklist item
1. In the Desktop Client, right-click the checklist, and click Create Check List Item in the shortcut menu. The Create New Check List Item dialog box is displayed.
 You can also create a new item on the Manage Checklist Items dialog box; see above.
2. Type a name and description for the new checklist item.
3. Click Finish.
Edit a check list
1. To edit a check list, select Manage Check List Items in the shortcut menu.
2. To add a new item to the list, click 
.
3. To move the position of an item in the list, select the item, and then use 
 or 
.
4. To delete an item, select the item, and then click 
.
5. To change an item’s description, click the description, and type the new instructions.
Copy a check list
1. Right-click the check list in the Desktop Client, and then click Copy in the shortcut menu.
2. In the Name box, type a new name for the check list.
3. Click Finish.
Email definition configuration
For more information about configuration email definitions, see Configuring email notification definitions in the How to Configure the Infrastructure Model section.
Create a reason for receipt
Reasons for receipt allow you to define why a user is receiving an assignment, such as work, information only, and comment. This object is used on the transmittal matrix assignments and workflow step recipient forms. For more
information about configuring the transmittal functionality, see Setting up transmittals.
1. Select File > New > Workflow > Reason for Receipt to display the New Reason For Receipt dialog box.
2. Type a unique name and description for the reason for receipt.
 The description is used in the Reason For Receipt (RFR) column item in the To Do list column set and so should not be left blank.
3. Select the Information only option if the reason for receiving the transmittal will be only for informational purposes.
4. Click Finish to create the new reason for receipt.
Workflow template configuration
Workflow templates contain the steps necessary to achieve a change management process. A workflow administrator creates workflow templates consisting of a set of configured workflow steps with defined recipients. Each step defines
an action that a user or the software must complete for the object to move to the next step in the workflow. Steps can optionally have a list of checklist items that the user must complete and check off before the object moves to the next
step.
These templates are conditionally made available to manage different classes of object. Shortcut commands on objects in the desktop client are used to send them down a workflow and then manage the item in that workflow.
The order of processing for each step is defined by the Accept and Reject steps. By default, the first step is started when the workflow is attached to the change object. Subsequent steps start when the prerequisite steps are completed.
Parallel steps are configured by specifying a number of steps that start based upon the completion of a particular step.
The steps in the workflow template are selected from a library of step definitions to ensure a consistent look and feel to all workflows. The following information can be specified on the steps in the workflow template:
Option to utilize e-mail notification as well as the To Do List.
Option to provide a condition to filter attachment of object. For example, a document would have to be working to be submitted to an approval/signoff workflow.
Step dependency (allowing for serial or parallel processing).
The recipients of each step (these can be users, roles, or matrices).
The next step for voting steps with a voting percentage that determines a true vote by all recipients.
The object status on step completion.
A rejection route for validation or voting steps.
The step duration (used to set the target completion date when the workflow is in use).
Optional checklists can be configured and attached for work assignment steps. 
Creating a new workflow template
When creating a custom workflow template, there are a number of tasks that you must do, such as building the workflow template from existing workflow steps and associating it with interfaces that represent items that can use this
workflow.
Additional tasks may be performed, depending on how custom you want the workflow to be. For example, you may have to create custom workflow steps to include in the workflow template. The following diagram illustrates the process of
creating a custom workflow template.
 Click a box in the diagram to go to the associated procedure.
Create or update a workflow template
Before creating a new workflow template, you need to create any new step classes, definitions, statuses, check lists, and reasons for receipt that will be required. For more information about the process of creating a new custom workflow
template, see Creating a new workflow template.
 To create or update a workflow, users must be a member of the WorkflowAdmin group.
 The same form is used for both creating and updating workflow templates.
1. In the Desktop Client, select File > New > Workflow > Workflow Template.
You can also choose the workflow in the Desktop Client, and select Update in the shortcut menu.
2. Type a unique name and description for the new workflow template.
This form is also available in several other ways as well. It can also be used to update a workflow template or to show details.
You can also edit and view instantiated workflows with this form.
This means any changes that can be made to a template can be made to an instantiated workflow with the following exceptions:
An instantiated workflow has no condition.
It is not possible to alter the transmittal issue step on an instantiated workflow.
It is not possible to change a step that has already started. 
3. To display the Reassign to Participant list, type * or other suitable search criteria in the Reassign to Participant search box, select Find, and then select the participant from the list.
The Submitter represents the person who submitted the object to the workflow.
If multiple recipients are assigned to a step and even one recipient rejects the step, then the object in the workflow is sent back to the user specified in the Reassign to Participant property in the workflow template. However, if
you use a voting step to calculate the number of accept and reject responses, you can set a threshold that, when met, allows the workflow to continue with the correct accept or reject path, rather than being reassigned to
another user.
A step is not reassigned to a participant if it has a single success step and that success step is not a voting step.
4. To display the Conditions list, enter * or other suitable search criteria in the Condition search box, select Find, and choose a condition from the list.
5. If you want to disable all e-mail notifications to the recipients associated with this workflow, select the No E-mail check box.
6. If you want to automatically delete any completed or terminated workflow instances, select the Delete Workflows On Completion option.
 You must attach the workflow template to an interface definition so the workflow is available for all the objects or classes associated with the interface definition.
Create workflow steps
1. To create the first workflow step, select Insert Before. The first step is displayed in the step list.
 To see a graphical representation of the workflow at any point, select View. For more information, see Viewing workflows.
2. Select a Step Definition. The Name box is populated automatically with the step name.
3. In the Duration box, type the number of days allocated for this step. The step duration is used to calculate the target completion date, which is then used to perform overdue notifications and workflow expirations.
4. Beside the Recipients box, select 
 to display the Update Step Recipient form.
5. Select the recipients that this step will be assigned to, and select OK to return to the Create Template form.
For more information, see Assign recipients to a workflow step. (Recipients may be users, roles, or determined by a matrix.)
 Some types of steps, such as a voting step, do not have recipients. In this case the Recipients button is disabled.
6. If a checklist is to be attached to this step, select one in the Checklist box.
7. If you want to force the recipient to complete the check list before they can complete the step, select the Completed check box.
 If the check box is not selected, the recipient will have the option to complete none, some, or all the check list items.
8. To set the Accept Status (that is, the status of the workflow object if the step is completed successfully), select 
 beside the Accept Status box, and choose the status from the list.
9. To select the Accept Percentage (percentage of workflow completed with the successful completion of this step), select 
 beside the Accept Percentage box.
 Select the Accept Percentage for each workflow status in incremental order.
10. To set the Reject Status (that is, the status of the workflow object if the step is rejected), select 
 beside the Reject Status box, and choose the status from the list.
11. To select the Reject Percentage (percentage of workflow completed with the failure of this step), select 
 beside the Reject Percentage box.
 Select the Reject Percentage for each workflow status in incremental order.
12. To add the next step, choose the current step, and select Insert After.
13. Repeat steps 2 to 11 above for each step you insert.
14. To arrange the steps:
Select Insert After to add a new step after the current selection.
Select Insert Before to add a new step before the current selection.
Select Remove to delete the current selected step.
To change the order of the steps, select a step and use 
 and 
 to move it up or down in the list.
You can also use Auto Order to let SmartPlant Foundation select the best order. This will take the first step in the grid and assume it is the first step in the workflow. The software will then navigate the accept steps to find a
good order to display the steps in. Provisions have been made to keep a branch in a parallel workflow together.
15. For each step in the workflow except the last one, set the Accept Step or the Reject Step. See also Workflow accept and reject steps for details.
All steps except the last one must have an accept step.
Depending on the outcome of the step (voting, accept, reject) the objects in the workflow will follow either the Accept or Reject path. You can specify both accept and reject paths.
16. Click OK to save the new workflow template.
An example of a completed workflow template is shown below.
Accept and Reject Percentage
The Accept Percentage and Reject Percentage values indicate what percent the workflow is completed. You use the Accept percentage box for voting steps in the Select Accept Steps dialog box, which opens when you select the
Accept Step cell. You specify how many voters must approve a step before the workflow can proceed to the Accept Step. For more information about configuring voting steps, see Set a voting workflow step
Delete completed workflows on completion
When you create or update a workflow template, you can select an option to automatically delete all completed or terminated instantiated workflows for that template in your system. This ensures that the workflow history for an associated
object is cleared from that point onwards for any newly instantiated workflows when the workflow completes or terminates. This can help reduce your database growth and maintain your systems performance.
1. In the Desktop Client, select File > New > Workflow > Workflow Template.
 You can also choose a workflow in the Desktop Client, and select Update in the shortcut menu.
2. In the Update Workflow Template window, select the Delete Workflows On Completion option to automatically delete any completed or terminated workflow instances in the system from that point.
Depending on your business needs, you can choose to set this option to safely remove any newly associated workflow instances from the database from that point onwards. You can turn off this feature to keep any new completed or
terminated workflow instances when the data is needed, such as for audit or regulatory purposes.
Pre-existing workflow instances in the database prior to selecting this feature are not deleted. This ensures only newly instantiated workflows from that point onwards are automatically deleted.
Associate a workflow template with an interface
Once you have created a custom workflow template, it must be associated with an interface to specify what types of objects can use the new workflow. For more information about configuring custom workflow templates, see Creating a
new workflow template.
To associate the workflow template to a type of object, find an interface definition that will be instantiated by just that type of object (Find > Schema > Interface Definition), and then drag the workflow template onto the interface definition.
Lock down a workflow template
You can lock down a workflow template so that it cannot be edited or used by instantiating the ISPFNonEditableCopy interface definition onto the workflow template. This is useful if you want to keep a workflow template as a master and
use it only for reference.
Elements used in the workflow template master realize the ISPFNonEditableCopy interface definition, which does not allow those elements to be referenced, edited, updated, or terminated.
You cannot use the workflow template master in the user interface as the selection or update options are disabled. A workflow template master must be named with MSR as a prefix to indicate that it is a template master.
If you want to use or edit a workflow template master, you must copy it. The copy of the workflow template does not instantiate the ISPFNonEditableCopy interface definition, so you can edit and update the workflow template copy to
modify the workflow process. The copy can be used and selected in the user interface so objects can be attached.
The workflow template copy should be named with HEX as a prefix to indicate that it is the editable version of the workflow.
Workflow step definitions
Transmittal issue step
Used to identify a step to pre-expand the recipients when the workflow starts. It enables you to see who the recipients will be before you run a report and is only used in transmittal workflows on the issue step.
 The Transmittal Issue Step box is only displayed when the Transmittal Workflow check box is selected.
Reassign to participant
The Reassign to Participant determines who a step is reassigned to if the workflow is trying to reject, or can’t reject, a step.
When a work step has no one assigned to it for work purposes when the participants are expanded, the step is then assigned to the Reassign to Participant.
If a step is rejected and it is in parallel or it has no rejection path set, it is not possible to move the workflow on. In that case, the step is also reassigned to the Reassign to Participant, so that they can action the step.
A step is not reassigned to a participant if it has a single success step and that success step is not a voting step.
Condition
A condition determines when you can put an object into the workflow.
Before an object can be attached to a workflow, the template must be related to an interface that is instantiated by the object (this is done through a drag and drop operation). The system tests against the condition if one is provided. If the
condition is met, the workflow is attached.
No email
This option will disable all email notifications to the recipients associated with this workflow.
Assign recipients to a workflow step
A dedicated GUI (Update Step Recipients form) is used to configure the recipients of a workflow step. Recipients may be users, roles, or matrices. Buttons are provided to query for users, roles, or matrices. The results of the search are
added to the tree view on the left so you can drag them into the assignment or information set of recipients.
In the Create Workflow Template or Update Workflow Template form, beside the Recipients box, click 
 to display the Update Step Recipients form.
 After upgrade, when Group assignment control or Group acknowledgement control is set to OneUserPerRole, all the workflow steps that are assigned to the role are split into separate workflow steps for each user.
Update the Step Recipients form
This form allows you to specify the users, roles, or distribution matrices assigned to the step, as well as the action required from each user or role. For users or roles who receive paper copies of assignments, you can also specify the print
format.
Buttons are provided to query for users, roles, or matrices. The results of the search are added to the tree views on the left for dragging into the assignment, information, or matrix set of recipients.
The upper left pane displays the users and roles available to add to the step.
To populate the list, perform a query by clicking the User or the Role button and defining search criteria.
The lower left pane displays the matrices available to add to the step.
To populate the list, perform a query by clicking the Matrices button and defining search criteria.
Assignment list
The Assignment list displays the users or roles assigned to the step for an action (such as work or comment).
The User or Role column displays the users or roles who have been assigned a step action.
Select a user or role in the tree in the left pane, and click Add.
The description field is populated automatically when the recipient is added. Click in the cell to edit the description.
You can also select a recipient in the left pane and drag it into the assignment pane.
If the associated step is information only, you will not be able to populate the assignment set.
The action Reason for receipt column determines the actions required from the user or role. This cell is populated automatically from the default value set. Click in the cell to select a different value from the list.
The default assignment values supplied are:
W (work)
C (comment)
Review complete
The Format column determines the document print format for users who receive paper documents. When you add a recipient, this cell is populated with the default format value. To change the default value, click in the cell to display the
format list.
Matrix Name column – if a matrix is on the step and it is expanded out when the workflow is run, the users or roles in that matrix will appear in this column to show where they came from.    
Information only list
The Information only list displays the users or roles assigned to the step for information purposes. The check box beside each user or role determines whether a response is required upon receipt of the workflow step.
To add a recipient to the Information only list, select a user or role in the tree in the left pane, and click Add (you can also drag it to the recipient in the list).
The Information only columns are the same as for the Assignment columns except the User or Role name has a response check box beside it.
If you do not want the user to acknowledge the information step, clear the check box beside the recipient's name. (The check box is selected by default when you add a recipient to the Information only list.)
The default information Reason for receipt value is I.
Matrices list
To add a matrix to the matrix list, select a matrix in the tree in the lower left pane, and click Add. (You can also drag the matrix onto the list.)
Default values
Use the drop-down lists in the bottom left of the form to set the default values for the:
Format
Action reason for receipt
Information reason for receipt
Group acknowledgement control
If a role is assigned to a step as information only, select one of the options in the Group acknowledgement control.
One user per step - Only one user can acknowledge receipt of the step.
One user per role - Any one of the assignees in each role can acknowledge receipt. If a user acknowledges the step, the steps for the other users assigned to that role are acknowledged automatically.
All recipients - All assignees must acknowledge receipt.
Group assignment control
If one of the Assignment recipients is a role, select one of the options in the Group assignment control.
OneUserPerStep - Only one user can perform the step task.
OneUserPerRole - Any one of the assignees in each role can perform the task but they first must claim it in order prevent other recipients from duplicating the work. If a user claims the step, the steps for the other users assigned to
that role are signed off automatically.
AllRecipients - All assignees must perform the task before it completes.
 If multiple recipients are assigned to a step and even one recipient rejects the step, then the object in the workflow is sent back to the user specified in the Reassign to Participant property in the workflow template. However, if you
use a voting step to calculate the number of accept and reject responses, you can set a threshold that, when met, allows the workflow to continue with the correct accept or reject path, rather than being reassigned to another user.
Transfer ownership of items from one user to another
When a user is permanently reassigned, such as when they leave a company, a Business Administrator needs to transfer ownership of additional items the user owns, which can include documents, subscriptions, matrices, and much
more. Notifications must also be sent of the change of ownership. For more information on how to transfer ownership to another user, see Reassign tasks owned by one user to another user.
To permit this transfer of ownership, SPFAllowRelReassignment is exposed on IRelDef2 Interface, and is set on the following relationships in the SmartPlant Foundation data model:
SPFItemOwner
SPFNotifyUser
SPFWorkflowReassignToUser
SPFRecipientUser
SPFUserReassignmentDeputyUser
SPFRelReassignmentCondition is a relationship definition used on IRelDef and on ISPFCondition that checks that certain conditions exist before items are reassigned. The minimum cardinality is 1 and the maximum is * on the relationship
for End1. The minimum cardinality is 0 and the maximum is 1 on the relationship for End2.
The following conditions are set automatically to check for items to be reassigned.
IsWorkflowStepValidToReassign – A condition on SPFRecipientUser relationship definition determines if workflow steps are on a workflow template, are in a live workflow and not ready, or ready to start, and, therefore, can be
reassigned
IsDeputyInActiveReassignmentPeriod - A condition on SPFUserReassignmentDeputyUser relationship definition to check if the reassignment period set on the original user is valid and not past dates.
SPFUserReassignmentDeputyUser is a relationship definition used on ISPFUserReassignment and ISPFLoginUser to show an administrator the projects for which ownership of a user's items has been reassigned and additional projects
for which ownership of a user's items needs to be reassigned.
Viewing workflows
The graphical view command is available using a button in the Create and Update Workflow Template forms. It is also available by right-clicking an object in the workflow client. The graphical view is useful for showing parallel steps.
The following symbols are used to represent objects in the workflow:
Step shape
Type
Symbol
Information
I
Process
P
Sign Off
S
Vote Condition
V
Work Assignment
W
1. In the Create or Update Workflow Template form, click View.
The workflow displayed is read only; you cannot edit the workflow in this window.
Each node represents a step in the workflow, and the status of the step is represented by a color.
To identify whether a step in the workflow is completed automatically or by a user, an additional workflow status, Auto Signed Off, is used in the workflow graphical viewer window. For more information, see View auto signed off
workflow steps.
2. To change the color assigned to each status, click the colored box associated with the status color to be changed. In the example above, to change the Ready to Start status from yellow to another color, click on the yellow box to the
left of the Ready to Start status to display the color selector, select a different color, and click OK.
 Any changes to the colors will be applied to all the templates.
3. To see the properties for a step, right-click on the step, and then click on Display Properties in the shortcut menu.
4. To change the information displayed on the symbols, click Fields to display the Set Attributes to Display form.
Use the column set box to change which properties are displayed on the symbols.
The default is WFVColumnSet, which displays the step name, step description, step target date, and comments.
5. Select the Display Property Name check box to show the property names.
6. Click Close.
Workflow accept and reject steps
With the exception of the first step in a workflow, each step must have a preceding step.
Accept step – the next step to be performed if the current step is accepted.
Reject step -the next step to be performed if the current step is rejected or fails.
 Ensure that the first step in the workflow is correct before you set the Accept and Reject steps. This first step is the only one that does not have a preceding step.
A workflow can be accepted or rejected to another workflow.
When you set an Accept or Reject step, you are setting the next step in the workflow.
If you reject a step in a workflow, and the reject path is a future step in the same workflow, a new revision of the workflow is not created. The workflow object continues in the original revision of the workflow, optimizing the use of the
database.
Set an accept workflow step
1. In either the Create Workflow Template or Update Workflow Template forms, select the step for which you want to set the accept path.
2. Beside the Accept Step box, select 
 to display the Select Accept Steps form.
The left pane displays the steps in the current workflow.
Use Find to display different workflows. You can link a step in one workflow to a step in another.
3. In the left pane, select the next step to be performed if this step is accepted.
4. Select Add. The step is displayed in the right pane.
5. Repeat steps 3 and 4 for any parallel steps that are required.
6. Select OK.
Set a reject workflow step
1. In either the Create or Update Workflow Template forms, select the step you want to set as the reject path.
 For more information, see Workflow template configuration.
2. Beside the Reject Step box, select 
 to display the Reject Accept Steps form.
The left pane displays the steps in the current workflow
Use Find to display different workflows. You can link a step in one workflow to a step in another.
3. In the left pane, select the next step to be performed if this step is rejected.
4. Select Add. The step is displayed in the right pane.
5. Select OK.
Set a voting workflow step
Voting is a step for processing the results from previous approval steps.
1. Select the voting step in the left pane.
 The left pane displays the steps in the current workflow. You can use Find to display different workflows.
2. Set the Accept percentage, and select Add.
 This accept value is the percentage of the votes needed to allow the workflow step to continue to the Accept Step. If the voting acceptance percentage limit is not reached, as there are more rejects than accepts, the
workflow waits for each parallel step to be completed before sending it onto the Reject path.
View auto signed off workflow steps
The Auto Signed Off status applies to steps that have not been approved interactively but have been accepted automatically.
For example, if a step being rejected or accepted has resulted in a second revision of the workflow, the first step to be used in the revised workflow may not be the actual first step in the next workflow. All steps before the selected first step
in the next or revised workflow are set to the Auto Signed Off status.
Select View on the Details for workflow template window. Any steps before the rejected step, which have not been approved, display as automatically signed off.
Configure attaching a workflow
By default, the Run in asynchronous mode parameter on the AttWFlow method is set to NULL, which means that when you attach a single object to a workflow, the attach action is immediately processed synchronously, and you then
must wait for the attach action to finish before you can perform other tasks. When you attach multiple objects to a workflow, the software creates the relationships asynchronously in the background, allowing you to continue working.
By changing the value of the Run in asynchronous mode parameter defined for the method you are using to attach workflows, you can change this behavior:
When you set the Run in asynchronous mode parameter to True, both single objects and multiple objects are attached to a workflow asynchronously in the background. This creates a notification object for each attach action so
you can view the progress and continue working.
When you set the Run in asynchronous mode parameter to False, both single objects and multiple objects are attached to a workflow synchronously and you must wait for the attach process to finish before you can continue
working.
As mentioned previously, when the Run in asynchronous mode parameter is set to NULL, single objects are processed synchronously, but multiple objects are processed asynchronously.
Workflow access configuration
Access to workflows might need to be restricted if the user who is trying to access the data is not meant to view it. For example, an external contractor should not be able to see any transmittals that are not intended for his company.
Access can be restricted by relating the user role to an object class and setting a condition on the relationship.
Objects attached to workflows are related to roles using the SPFClassDefRoleSplitCondition relationship. Conditions can be set on the SPFClassDefRoleSplitCondition relationship using another relationship, SPFRoleSplitConditionDef. A
linked interface, ISPFRoleSplitCondition, is used to link the SPFClassDefRoleSplitCondition relationship with the condition. For example, if Obj1 is an object attached to a workflow and Obj2 is a user, then you can validate the user
organization against the organization of the attached object with the condition: Obj1->SPFCXmtlExternalCompany_12.Name=Obj2->SPFUserOrganization_12.
Restrict users from accessing objects attached to a workflow
1. Find the class definition of the object that will be placed into a workflow, but that you want to restrict access to.
2. Right-click the class definition, and select Manage Role Evaluation Condition.
3. Select a condition definition from the Condition Definition list.
4. Click OK.
 The condition can also be set on the selected role using the Manage Class Definition Evaluation Condition command.
Distribution matrix configuration
Distribution matrices are used to set up lists of recipients for workflow steps based on properties or relationships of the object in the workflow. Each recipient on the matrix has a Reason For Receipt (RFR), which indicates why the recipient
is included.
A distribution matrix is a table with recipients along the top and matrix categories down the side. The matrix categories can be based on any property or relationship on the object going down the workflow, such as document type, project,
and contract. Each cell in the table is marked with the reason for receipt for each recipient.
Distribution matrices determine which users receive the workflow actions and what tasks are required of those recipients. Separate matrix classes must be defined for each object that has different mappings.
Alternative matrices can be configured on a step and controlled by a condition. For example, transmittal matrices are created for a reason for issue and related to the step by a condition such as IsIssuedForConstruction.
Configure a distribution matrix
1. Model the matrix class.
Ensure the matrix class realizes an interface that implies ISPFDistributionMatrix.
2. Model the row class.
3. Define a relationship between the two primary interfaces of the matrix and matrix row classes as a specialization of SPFDistributionMatrixRow.
4. Model the row mapping.
Create an interface for the mapping properties. Make sure this interface implies the ISPFDistributionMapRow interface and is realized by the matrix row class.
Create the mapping properties – these will appear as columns in the matrix table.
Map these properties to the properties and/or relationships on the object that will be going down the workflow.
 Multiple cardinality relationships can be used to map a workflow item to matrix rows. If the object in the workflow is related to two or more items by a row mapping relationship, then all the related rows will be selected when
the matrix is evaluated, and users on each selected matrix row will be included as step recipients.
For the full schema diagrams for the transmittal matrices, see Default workflow templates.
Use a distribution matrix
When a user right-clicks on a matrix in the Desktop Client, the matrix is built by retrieving the mapping properties for the matrix row. These are on the interfaces implied by ISPFDistributionMapRow. Each property on the matrix row
interface is mapped to a property or relationship on the object in the workflow.
1. Create the matrix.
2. Assign recipients to the matrix. See Matrix management.
3. Relate the matrix to the workflow step. See Assign recipients to a workflow step.
System evaluation of recipients for a workflow step
If one or more matrices are related to a workflow step, when it starts, the system tests the step matrix condition against the object in the workflow to determine which one to use. If one matches, the mapping properties for the matrix row are
determined as above. The values for the matrix look up are then retrieved from the object by traversing from the matrix mapping properties to the related object properties and/or relationship. These values are used to identify the matrix row
to use.
External and internal transmittal matrices and a document review matrix are delivered with the product workflow model. Other distribution matrices can be configured, as required, by producing a new matrix row class definition with new
mapping interfaces.
Matrix management
Matrices are created like any other object. A special command on the shortcut menu of a matrix is used to populate the matrix, create, update, and delete matrix rows as required.
Create a new matrix
1. Select File > New > Workflow > <Type of> Matrix in the Desktop Client. The New <Type of> Matrix form is displayed.
2. Type a name and description for the matrix.
3. Click Finish.
Off-line matrices
Many companies have standard distribution matrices that are imported into the system as part of project initiation. The Assign Recipients GUI can be used to create these off-line matrices and import them into other projects.
Assign matrix recipients
The Assign Recipients form is used to specify the users or roles included in the selected distribution matrix, as well as the action required from each user or role upon receipt of object in the workflow. For users or roles who receive paper
copies of workflow objects, you can also specify the page size.
1. To assign the recipients to the matrix, right-click the matrix in the Desktop Client, and click Assign Recipients in the shortcut menu.
If an internal matrix is selected, the Assign Recipients form will display two columns, Doc category and Contract, as in the following diagram.
2. Click 
 to create a new row.
3. Click in the first cell in the row, and select or enter the properties for the row criteria.
 You can use the asterisk (*) wildcard character, alone or with other characters, to associate a row with multiple values of a property. For example, if you specify *process* for the document category property, the row of the
matrix will be applicable for any document category that contains process, such as Process Documents or Process Controlled Documents.
4. Use the User and Role buttons to populate the recipient list in the left pane.
5. To assign a recipient, such as a user or role, to the matrix, select the recipient in the recipient list, and click Add. A column is added for the new recipient. The new column is automatically populated with the default Reason for Receipt
and Format values if they have been set.
 You may also drag a recipient into the matrix pane.
6. To select different Reason for Receipt and Format values, select the cell, right-click, and select Reason for Receipt and Format from the shortcut menu.
7. Click 
 beside the Reason for receipt box, and select a reason from the list.
 If you select I from the Reason for receipt list, the recipient will receive the step for information only, and no action is required.
8. Click 
 beside the Format for receipt box, and select a format from the list.
9. Repeat steps 1-8 for each recipient you add to the matrix.
10. Click OK.
An example matrix is shown below.
The following options are available:
Color palettes - Allows you to customize the cell colors.
Users - Allows you to search for users to assign to the distribution matrix.
Roles - Allows you to search for roles to assign to the distribution matrix.
Default format - Specifies the paper format automatically assigned to newly added paper users.
Default reason for receipt - Specifies the reason for receipt automatically assigned to newly added users or roles.
Size columns for best fit - Resizes the columns in the matrix view.
Validate matrix - Allows you to validate property values in the cells of the matrix against the drop-down list options, before creating any related items in the system.
 With the Validate matrix check box selected, the values in the cells of the matrix are validated; any differences against the drop-down list options are marked as in Error. You cannot save the recipient matrix; a
message asks you to ensure that the data entered is valid. Switching this option off avoids these errors and allows you to import data, for example from Microsoft Excel, and populate the matrix.
Right-click a column heading to display a shortcut menu with the following options:
Move Column - Adjusts the order in which the user or role columns are displayed.
Add Column - Allows you to add a user or role column. You must type a valid user or role name to add the new column.
Duplicate Column - Allows you to add a new user or role column with the same reasons for receipt as the column being duplicated.
Clear Column - Removes all reasons for receipt from the column.
Delete Column - Removes the user or role from the display.
Change Recipient - Allows you to change the recipient.
Right-click a row heading to display a shortcut menu with the following options:
Duplicate Row - Adds a new row with the same properties and reasons for receipt as the row being duplicated.
Delete Row - Removes the row from the display.
Export recipients
Recipients can be exported from the Assign Recipients form to the Clipboard.
1. In the Assign Recipients form, right-click in the top left cell, and click Export to Clipboard on the shortcut menu.
2. Open Excel.
3. Select a cell, and click Edit > Paste.
Import recipients
Recipients can be imported into the Assign Recipients from other applications using the clipboard. For example, to import data from Excel:
1. Select the data in Excel, and click Edit > Copy.
2. In the Assign Recipients form, right-click in the top left cell, and click Import from Clipboard on the shortcut menu.
Workflow processing
When a user attaches a workflow to an object, the following processing takes place.
Attach workflow to object
The system checks which workflows (if any) can be attached and presents a list to the user.
When a workflow is selected, the system copies the entire workflow template and attaches the copy to the object.
The system identifies the start step.
The system sets the initial workflow status (as set in workflow options).
If the attach and edit workflow option has been selected, the system makes the edit GUI available (can only edit target dates, step descriptions, and recipients).
For first step only
The following actions are implemented by the system only for the first step of the workflow.
Set target completion dates for each step in the workflow, based on the duration of each step in workflow.
Target date = Step start date + Step duration
This date initiates overdue management and step expiration.
Email users assigned to the first step.
Email submitter (to inform them the workflow has started).
If any recipient is a role, expand the role (unless it is an issue step). 
For all the steps
The system carries out the following actions when the step starts:
Get the step recipients.
If it is a process step, get the process class name, and run the process.
If it is a sign off step (a form of process step), call the appropriate server function to change the status of the document from Working to Current.
If sign off and notify, send an email notification to all the users that were subscribed to the document being signed off.
Email the recipients, as required, for:
Information only step - information only (may also include an action, such as Acknowledge).
Work step - action required.
 Emails are not sent if there is no email definition or if the No e-mail indicator is set.
During step duration:
If the step is claimed, remove the step from the To Do List of the other recipients.
If the user selects 'Unable to complete', follow the rejection process – see Workflow rejection and revision process.
The scheduler runs to check if:
overdue date has been reached (target date + allowable days overdue)
expiration date has been reached
If the overdue date has been reached, send overdue notification email at predetermined intervals (set in workflow options).
If expiration date has been reached, send notification email and reassign the step. See the Workflow expiration processing.
Claiming a workflow step resets target dates
When a workflow step is assigned one or more roles or multiple people, users can claim the step using Claim Workflow Step on the shortcut menu. The method used, WFClaim, has an argument called Reset Target Dates that, when
enabled, causes the recalculation of the target date of the step, as well as subsequent affected steps, based on the claim date and duration of the step. If this argument is disabled, claiming a step will have no effect on any dates. The
default value of this argument is Enabled.
Step sign off
The validation process is initiated when the step is completed (for example, by user acceptance). The sign off mechanism is the same for manual and process steps.
If the password required indicator is set on the step, then the user will be asked to enter a password to complete the step.
Check that all other steps (parallel steps) feeding into the step have been completed (unless they are a reject step).
A preceding information step that has not been acknowledged but has the 'Proceed without acknowledgement’ flag set will be accepted.
If the step is a voting step, check that the required percentage of votes has been exceeded. If it has not, then follow the rejection process – see the Workflow rejection and revision process.
If a step has a checklist attached with the mandatory indicator set, check if the list has been completed. If yes, then set who completed on the check list.
Target dates are updated for downstream steps that are in the Ready to Start status.
Step completion
If all above actions are passed, then the system:
Runs any post processing class.
Sets the step completion date.
Sets the name of the user who completed the step.
Sets the status of the next step to ‘Ready to start’.
Workflow completion
When all the workflow steps have been completed, the system:
Read the count of the number of workflows on the object and decrements it by 1.
Sets the workflow completed status (set in the workflow options).
Terminates the workflow and all its associated objects.
 This helps improve performance for workflow actions as the amount of live data is reduced.
Workflow processing option
A user can attach a workflow to an object when it is created. However, that functionality is turned off by default. To turn on the functionality, you must set the workflow processing option on the create method of the object.
1. Query for the create method for the class (Find > Administration > Method).
2. Update the method (right-click on the method, and select Update).
3. Enter a value for Workflow processing option. Possible values are:
Name
Desktop Client
Web Client
None
No Workflow page appears in the wizard, and the object is not attached to a workflow.
No Workflow page appears in the wizard, and the object is not attached to a
workflow.
Specific Workflow Name
No Workflow page appears in the wizard, and the object is attached to the named workflow.
No Workflow page appears in the wizard, and the object is attached to the
named workflow.
Select
The Workflow page appears in the wizard, but the user does not have to select a workflow to attach to the
object. It is optional.
The Workflow page appears in the wizard, but the user does not have to select
a workflow to attach to the object. It is optional.
SelectRequired
The Workflow page appears in the wizard, and the user must select a workflow to attach to the object.
The Workflow page appears in the wizard, and the user must select a workflow
to attach to the object.
If more than one workflow is identified, the software does not attach the object
to any workflow.
4. Select Apply or Finish to save your changes.
Attaching a default workflow
You can create methods to attach a specific workflow to new documents or other objects, without making your user select a workflow definition from the Attach and Edit Workflow dialog box. By configuring a method to use a specific
workflow definition, all new objects created with that method will automatically be attached to that workflow.
 For existing documents and objects, users must select a workflow definition from the Attach and Edit Workflow dialog box.
1. Create a new method using the AttachWorkflow client API, or edit an existing method based on that client API. For more information, see AttachWorkflow client API.
2. In the Workflow processing option box, type the name or UID of the workflow definitions that should automatically be associated with the object using this command.
3. If necessary, provide text in the User notification message box to customize the confirmation dialog box that will appear to the user when the object has been placed in the workflow.
Workflow options
Workflow options are implemented via the Find administration sub menu options. They provide configurable e-mail notifications that are distributed.
Workflow options provide a mechanism to configure the default e-mail message sent to the recipients of a step. These are overridden by any e-mails linked to the step class.
When a new step is started in a workflow, the workflow engine determines if the step class (relating to the current step) has an associated e-mail message and uses that e-mail message to send out a workflow e-mail notification. If no e-
mail message is found, then the default e-mail message is generated from the Options command.
Update workflow options
1. Open the system options, and look for the Workflow options details section. For more information on the system options, see Configure the system options.
2. Select the Display the workflow information steps if you want to show the information steps.
3. Select the Allow workflow step auto reassignment to enable the automatic reassignment function. For more information, see Allow reassignment of workflow steps.
See the following sections for details on setting the following workflow options:
Workflow Expiration Options, see Workflow expiration processing.
Email Definition Options, see Email definition configuration.
Configuring the calendar, see Maintain your calendar.
Merging workflow process steps, see Merging workflow process steps.
Workflow expiration processing
A scheduler process runs checks against overdue actions and expiration dates.
Use the File > Perform Expiration Checks command to create the scheduler task.
The scheduler task checks the dates and sends an email reminder or reassigns the step.
The action is eventually reassigned.
All these timings are configurable.
Configure workflow expiration options
The workflow expiration options are set up in the system options. See Set up the workflow overdue and expiration process options for more information.
Workflow overdue process
When a workflow action is overdue (that is, the target date is reached), the To Do List icon turns red.
When the To Do List icon turns red, you can start the expiration process using the File > Perform Expiration Checks command, and create a new expiration scheduler task, if one does not already exist.
The scheduler task checks the dates and sends an e-mail reminder.
If the number of days to re-assign is reached, the step is re-assigned to:
The owner of the change object, if a change object is being passed down the workflow.
The workflow submitter.
Perform expiration checks
The scheduler is responsible for determining which workflow steps have expired. Follow the instructions below to create a scheduler task to perform workflow expiration checks.
1. Click File > Perform Expiration Checks.
 If an expiration checks scheduler task already exists, you are notified that the task already exists, and the task appears in the list view window.
2. Enter a start date and time for the task. Click the arrow 
 to select a date from the calendar.
3. Specify the number of times the scheduler task will run.
 Set Resubmission count to -1 to run an infinite number of times.
4. Specify how often the task will run. The Resubmission interval value is in milliseconds.
Create Expiration Checks Scheduler Task
Start date - the date and time the task will start
Resubmission count - how many times the scheduled task should be run. -1 indicates that a task will be run an infinite number of times.
Resubmission interval - how often the task should be run (in milliseconds).
Set up the workflow overdue and expiration process options
1. In the system options, scroll down to the Workflow options details section. For more information on the system options, see Configure the system options.
2. In the Allowable number of days to wait between e-mail box, type the number of days to wait between sending overdue reminder e-mails.
For example, if the interval is two days, the system will only send an e-mail reminder every two days instead of every day.
3. In the Allowable number of days overdue before expiration box, type the number of days a step can exceed its target date before the step is considered overdue. This number is used for the overdue steps report, and it controls
the color of the To Do List icon. For example, when a workflow action is overdue (that is, the target date is reached), the To Do List icon turns red. Only steps meeting the criteria entered here are considered in expiration checks.
4. In the Allowable number of days overdue before reassignment box, type the number of days a step can be overdue before it is reassigned.
5. When you have made all your selections, click Finish.
The remaining Workflow options are described below.
Workflow options details
Recalculate target dates on reassign - Recalculates the target date of a workflow step when you reassign that step to another user or role or when you add one or more users to that step. If you do not select this check box, the step
reassignment will have no effect on the target date of that step. This option is enabled by default when the product is upgraded to version 2019 and is disabled by default when version 2019 of the product is installed the first time.
Merge process steps on completion - When a workflow is completed or terminated all the consecutive process steps are merged into a single process step and the redundant steps are deleted. This can help reduce the database size.
Display workflow information steps - Displays any information steps in your To Do List. If you do not select this check box, the information steps are signed off automatically.
Allowable number of days to wait between e-mail - Allows you to set the number of days to wait between sending overdue reminder e-mails.
Allowable number of days overdue before expiration - Allows you to set the number of days a step can exceed its target date before the step is considered overdue. This number is used for the overdue steps report, and it controls the
color of the To Do List icon. For example, when a workflow action is overdue (that is, the target date is reached), the To Do List icon turns red. Only steps meeting the criteria entered here are considered in expiration checks.
Allowable number of days overdue before reassignment - Allows you to set the number of days a step can be overdue before it is reassigned.
 For more information about creating custom notifications to use in the following options, see Configuring email notification definitions.
Suppress all e-mail - Suppresses all e-mail notifications from workflow to recipients.
Reject e-mail definition - Sets the e-mail message sent to recipients when a step is rejected.
Action e-mail definition - Sets the e-mail message sent to the recipients when a step action is required.
Started e-mail definition - Sets the default e-mail message sent to the recipients of a step when that step is started. The delivery of this e-mail is optional.
Reassign to e-mail definition - Sets the e-mail message sent to the recipient to whom the step is reassigned.
Reassign from e-mail definition - Sets the e-mail message sent to the recipient from whom the step is reassigned.
First assignee e-mail definition - Sets the e-mail message sent to the first assignee.
Completion e-mail definition - Sets the e-mail message sent to the workflow submitter when the workflow is completed. The delivery of this e-mail is optional.
Sign off e-mail definition - Sets the e-mail message sent to recipients when a step is signed off.
Workflow off hold e-mail definition - Sets the e-mail message sent to recipients when the workflow is taken off hold.
Overdue e-mail definition - Sets the e-mail message sent to recipients when a step is overdue.
Workflow on hold e-mail definition - Sets the e-mail message sent to recipients when the workflow is put on hold.
Information e-mail definition - Sets the e-mail message sent to recipients when an information step is assigned to them.
Step claimed e-mail definition - Sets the e-mail message sent to the recipients when a step is claimed.
Future actions e-mail definition - Sets the e-mail message that is sent to all recipients of all future steps in a workflow when the first step has been completed.
Initial workflow status - Allows you to assign a status to a workflow step after the step is added to a workflow.
Completion workflow status - Allows you to assign a status to a workflow step after it is completed.
Information step definition - Allows you to select the step that is displayed in the inbox when a user is assigned as an Info Only recipient on an Assignment step.
Workflow calendar - Defines the default calendar to be used for workflows. For more information, see Configure and maintain calendars.
Allow workflow step auto reassignment - Enables the automatic reassignment function. For more information, see Allow reassignment of workflow steps.
Workflow viewer column set - Allows you to select the workflow properties to be displayed in the Workflow step details window in the Web Client.
Workflow rejection and revision process
When a workflow step is rejected:
1. If the rejection path is to a step in the same workflow:
A copy is made of the workflow and given the next revision number in the sequence. For example, if the original workflow was revision A, the copy will be revision B.
If the rejection path moves the object into a different workflow:
The new workflow is instantiated.
If no rejection path is defined:
The workflow is copied, as before, and it continues from the most recent assignment step.
2. The object is returned to the designated rejection step (in the new workflow).
 Any steps before the rejection step show as Auto Signed Off. See View auto signed off workflow steps for additional details.
3. Actions relating to the rejected step are removed from the user's To Do List.
4. The user's To Do List is populated with the actions relevant to the new step.
5. The workflow continues from the rejection step in the new copy (revision).
The following diagram shows an example of rejecting in the same workflow.
Merging workflow process steps
You have the option to merge a set of workflow process steps into a single process step to enhance the create workflow functionality. The multiple step classes merged into one process step are defined in the order of execution. You can
also merge all the workflow process steps when a workflow completes, and replace them with a single unified step.
The benefit of merging similar process steps into a single process step is that the database size may be significantly reduced for workflows that are used intensively.
Merge workflow process step type
This process step type merges a set of workflow process steps into one process step as you create a workflow process step definition. This enhances the create workflow functionality as you can merge multiple step classes in a defined
order of execution.
As you create the workflow definition, configure the SPFMergeProcessStepClasses relationship definition between the workflow step definition and each process step class in order, so that the process steps can be executed in the order
they are related.
Process class name: MergeProcessSteps
Syntax: MergeProcessSteps
Example: MergeProcessSteps
 For more information on how to use this merge workflow process type, see Create a merge workflow process step type.
Create a merge workflow process step type
You can create a merge workflow process step type using the MergeProcessSteps class to build one process step that executes a combined set of process steps defined in consecutive order.
To create a merge process workflow step type:
1. Create a workflow step definition, and in the Workflow Step Type tab, select the Process workflow step class.
2. Select the Is a merge process step option.
The Process class name box displays the MergeProcessSteps class name by default.
The Process steps box dynamically displays the available process steps you can add to the set. You can also search for other process steps to add.
3. Select the process steps and arrange them in the required order of execution.
 This merge process step type replaces the individual process steps and can help performance by reducing the database size.
Merge completed workflow steps
When a workflow completes, you have the option to automatically merge all completed workflow process steps and replace them with a single unified step. All the redundant steps are then deleted. The benefit of merging similar process
steps into a single process step on completion is that the database size may be significantly reduced for workflows that are used intensively.
You can configure a workflow template in two ways:
Go to an existing workflow template that has yet to complete, choose Update, and select the Merge process steps on completion option.
Before creating a new workflow template, in the System Options, scroll to the Workflow options details section, and check the Merge process steps on completion option.
 For more information, see Configure the system options.
Transmittals
Transmittals play an important part in tracking the progress of a project. Milestone payments from the client are often linked to the distribution of particular documentation on transmittals, the most notable of which is the site construction
transmittal when documents are released to the site for construction.
Transmittals are also useful for internal document management, such as the distribution of control documentation, safety procedures, and other documents between departments. This is typical of the owner operator (OO) as well as the
EPC.
What is a transmittal?
A transmittal is a controlled package of documents with an accompanying report. It is used to perform, record, and track the distribution of project documentation between the different design teams, subcontractors, vendors, and
construction sites involved in a project. The term transmittal usually refers to the top sheet or report that lists how many copies of each document are sent to whom, and for what reason.
In a paper-based system, the print room uses the transmittal to prepare physical prints. In an electronic, fully-integrated transmittal and document management system, such as SmartPlant Foundation, the transmittal is a collection object
that links a set of documents to a set of recipients, each with a reason for receipt. The transmittal report is then attached to the collection object and e-mailed to the recipients. In many cases, transmittals are used to distribute documents
between different companies, which would need to be recorded on the transmittal.
What happens when a transmittal is issued?
When a transmittal is issued, the attached documents are distributed to the list of recipients. In a paper-based environment, a transmittal report is sent to the print room, where the relevant documents are printed and packaged for
distribution. In an electronic system, the transmittal report is e-mailed to the recipients.
Recipients log on to SmartPlant Foundation to view the relevant documents, make comments, suggest changes, or take other actions, as necessary. For recipients who do not have access to SmartPlant Foundation, the documents must
be made available through some other mechanism, such as shared folders, FTP sites, or physical media. Their responses are then funneled back through a controlling recipient who gathers the responses and records them in SmartPlant
Foundation.
Transmittals that are issued for information are closed after issue. Transmittals that are issued for comment remain active until all comments are received, at which point the transmittal can be closed, usually by the document
controller.
Transmittals that are issued in error can be canceled. In this case a transmittal report is redistributed marked clearly as canceled as a form of recall notice.
How can distribution matrices help me manage the transmittal recipients?
Distribution matrices help you set up recipients lists for transmittals. You can create different matrices to manage distribution lists for internal and external transmittals. Their structures vary, but both include:
A Reason For Issue (RFI) for the transmittal, such as For Information or For Construction
A Reason for Receipt (RFR), such as For Comment, for each recipient
When using matrices, the system will first determine whether the transmittal is internal or external. Next it will determine if a matrix was created for the transmittal's Reason for Issue. Once the appropriate matrix is selected, the information
in the matrix will be used to create the recipient lists. For external matrices, the recipients are determined by the external organization the transmittal will be sent to. For internal matrices, the recipient list is created based on the contract the
transmittal was created for and the specific type of documents included.
Transmittal icons
Transmittal icons reflect the life cycle transmittal state and the transmittal class. They are also important in identifying an issued transmittal that is overdue (that is, its Planned Completion Date has been reached).
Internal
transmittal
icons
External
transmittal
icons
Transmittal state
RESERVED - The issue state is RESERVED after you enter transmittal details (such as planned
completion date), create additional transmittal sections as required, and select the transmittal
workflow.
ISSUED - The issue state is ISSUED after you run and attach the transmittal report, notify recipients
(e-mail or the SmartPlant Foundation To Do List), and set the transmittal Issue Date. Once the status
is changed to ISSUED, the transmittal structure is frozen.
ISSUED and Overdue - The planned completion date has been reached; however, the transmittal is
not completed.
COMPLETED - When all responses have been received and all issue steps are completed, the issue
state is set to COMPLETED. You cannot make any further updates or comments to the transmittal or
its report.
CANCELED - The issue state is CANCELED after you set the transmittal Cancel Date, User, and
Reason. In addition, if a transmittal is issued in error, the transmittal is reissued as CANCELED and
is redistributed as a form of recall notice. The transmittal structure is frozen.
TEMPLATE - The issue state TEMPLATE is a special state that indicates that the transmittal can be
used as a basis for new transmittals.
Transmittal project roles
This section lists typical user roles associated with transmittals. While roles may vary from organization to organization, the transmittal management process does include a number of standard roles.
Document controllers
These people are responsible for the integrity of all project documentation and the distribution of that documentation to the relevant project groups at the appropriate time. The following tasks are examples of responsibilities of the
document controller:
Project document index management
Transmittal distribution management
Transmittal preparation and distribution
Transmittal response tracking and follow up
Design engineers
These users are responsible for the generation of the project documentation. Although they do not manage transmittals themselves, they are typically responsible for the following kinds of tasks:
Review transmittal histories of documents
Prepare outline transmittals
Prepare transmittal issue requests
Receive and respond to transmittals
Clients
Client recipients must be clearly marked and distinguished from the main project recipients on most transmittals. They typically do not have access to the SmartPlant software directly. They have the following types of responsibilities:
Review transmittal histories of documents
Receive and respond to transmittals
Vendors and sub-contractors
These people are usually not connected to the project management system, but still exchange documentation with the project team, often in large batches. Transmittals are the mechanism for the exchange, but this process requires
packaging the documentation for distribution and access from a separate environment, often outside the firewall. These users typically participate in the following types of tasks:
Review transmittal histories of documents
Receive and respond to transmittals
Return project documentation on transmittals
Transmittal life cycle
The transmittal life cycle is summarized as follows:
Life cycle phase
Details
Creation
Provide transmittal details, such as planned completion date.
Create any additional transmittal sections, as required.
Select the transmittal workflow.
Create the transmittal with a RESERVED state.
Attach Documents
Add the required documents.
Manage Recipients
Add recipients manually or from a matrix.
Set recipient reasons for receipt, acknowledgement, and so forth.
Issue
Run and attach the transmittal report.
Generate alternate rendition and replace the original file.
Send notification to recipients (e-mail and/or SmartPlant
Foundation Inbox).
Set the transmittal IssueDate.
Set transmittal state to ISSUED.
Freeze the transmittal structure.
Track Responses
Reporting on outstanding action, overdue actions, and so forth.
Complete
Set the transmittal Completion Date.
Set the transmittal state to COMPLETED.
In addition to these standard life cycle events, a transmittal may also exist in the following states.
Template
This is a special state indicating that the transmittal can be used as a basis for new transmittals.
Cancel
Set the transmittal Cancel Date, User and Reason
Set the transmittal state to CANCELED
Freeze the transmittal structure
If cancelled from the ISSUED state, reissue to recipients as CANCELLED.
Transmittal structure
