instructions are created during publish to allow that object to be claimed into the project. To merge objects published to the project-level up to the plant level, merge the object to the as-built plant in the authoring environment and republish
it there.
Configuration hierarchy
The configuration hierarchy provides the structure for segregating working data from different, alternate designs from contractors and controls the merging of this data.
Configuration Top is the top node of the configuration hierarchy. Data and documents created here are configuration independent, so they are visible and available for update from any configuration.
Plant is the first level in the configuration hierarchy and usually contains the as-built data.
Work can be performed in the plant directly or created and merged up through the lower configurations. The plant is usually at level 1, which is directly below Configuration Top. However, each configuration can be configured to match your
specific needs. To integrate with any of the design tools, the plant must be at level 1 and project must be at level 2.
Configuration-dependent data
An object's class definition determines whether it is configuration-controlled or not. A configuration-controlled object is created in a specified node in the configuration hierarchy. For more information on setting query and create
configurations, see Creating configurations.
Queries are performed in the selected query configurations, as well as in the top configuration. For more information on queries, see Query configurations.
You can create a configuration controlled object in the Configuration Top, which makes it a configuration-independent object, visible from all configurations. For example, folders can be created by engineers as either project-specific objects
or plant-specific objects, or they can also be created by the administrators as system-wide objects.
 Configuration Top is configuration independent and so objects at that level cannot be claimed, even if the class definition is configured to be configuration controlled.
Configurations
There are several types of configurations in concurrent engineering that you can use to sort the data or claim objects as follows.
Data segregation
Data segregation by using configurations must not be confused with data segregation by using domains. Domains separate the data being created or published according to the authoring tool, (such as Smart P&ID) or the SmartPlant
Foundation feature from which it originates or in which it exists.
A configuration-controlled item can be hidden from any users by controlling their access to the configuration. With multiple projects running, this mean the users working on each of the projects can be prevented from seeing or modifying
another project's data, but they can all see the configuration independent data at the plant level. Users can only see another project's data if they are granted access to the other project.
Data and documents can be created in any configuration. However, before you can make isolated changes to data or documents, you must first claim them down into the relevant lower configuration. Before any changes can be merged
back up into the higher configuration as new data, you must first resolve any conflicts that have been found.
Claim items
To change any as-built data in a project, the items must first be claimed into the project.
When you claim data items, they are reserved for change within the project. When you claim documents, they are revised to create a new working revision within the project. When you claim items, any related items can be automatically
claimed as well, depending on their relationship configuration in the schema. Reference file management controls any related documents in a similar way.
SmartPlant Foundation can also be configured to control how users make parallel changes to the same item.
Exclusive Claim (Pessimistic) - When a user has claimed an item for their updates, no other user can claim, edit, or update that item until the claim has been released or the item has been merged back to the higher configuration.
Nonexclusive Claim (Optimistic) - Allows for parallel changes and is named Optimistic as this is when the user hopes that few conflicts are created when merging the project into the plant. Users are informed when a parallel
change is initiated, and conflict resolution helps to prevent the overwriting of data. The same data and documents can be changed in multiple projects.
There are two ways of establishing a non-exclusive claim:
Explicit claim - Where an item is first claimed into a project before any changes are made to the item at the project level.
Implicit claim - Where an item is automatically claimed into a project when you make a change to a plant item when working at the project level.
In implicit claim mode, items are automatically claimed into the user's active project as they are modified with commands, such as Update. However, this is possible only if those items are in the plant found above the project.
In the implicit mode, the user can chose to be notified of the claim before the update takes place.
You can configure SmartPlant Foundation to force all users to work in explicit mode. If this option is not set, users can elect to work in implicit mode.
Conflict resolution
After an object is claimed, conflicts can arise when you attempt to merge the object and the original object has changed in the higher configuration, either directly or through a merge from a parallel configuration, such as a design basis
change. It is not possible to merge an object into the higher configuration before the conflicts with the design basis have been resolved.
A Resolve Conflicts dialog box allows you to see all the differences between the two objects and then choose to:
Overwrite the values in the higher configuration during the merge, or
Update the values in the lower configuration
Once all conflicts are resolved, the claimed object can then be merged into the higher configuration.
Merge items
The process called Merging is when an object is created or changed in a lower configuration, and it is moved up into the parent configuration when it is complete.
When a lower configuration object is merged up to the parent configuration, the object at the parent configuration is updated with the values and relationships from the lower configuration object. Any related items may be automatically
merged depending on the relationship configuration in the schema.
 You can create a method using the MergeableItemSummary Client API to create a summary of items in a lower configuration that are ready to be merged to a higher configuration or plant. For more information on generating a
mergeable item summary, see MergeableItemSummary client API.
Merge items by grouping object
You can merge items into a parent configuration using a structured merge by grouping object. This structured merge can be configured in a new method that defines the path to locate the specific grouping object items found in the sub-
configurations. These grouped items are merged into the parent configuration without having to merge all the data available in a selected project. The merge of grouped objects starts with selecting a single grouping object, for example a
Commissioning Unit, System, or Area. There can be many levels that contain data located by the grouping object; the level and depth of data is controlled by a graph definition that can be configured in the method. Some items may or may
not be able to be merged, including the grouping object itself because it may already reside in a higher configuration. For more information on configuring a merge by grouping object, see Configuring merge by grouping.
Each merge by grouping object carries out merge validation before proceeding. Any conflicts or errors that occur are itemized in an exception report for resolution. For more information on reviewing grouping by object exceptions, see
Reviewing merge by grouping object errors.
Merging data using the merge by grouping feature does not support multiple grouping objects.
If a grouping object cannot be merged, it will not be terminated after the completion of a successful merge.
You can create a merge summary of items by grouping object before merging the selected data to a higher configuration using a method configured with the MergeStructureSummary client API. For more information, see
MergeStructureSummary client API.
Reports
There are five concurrent engineering reports available in SmartPlant Foundation.
View Future Changes Report - From the configuration top, or plant level, this report lists items in lower configurations that are being changed and reports where these items are located.
Non Mergeable Items Report - Lists items in a configuration that cannot be merged due to design basis conflicts.
Configuration Report - Lists all new items, changed items, and terminated items within a configuration, as well as their merge status.
Merge Progress Notification Error Report - Lists items directly related to the merge progress notification item that failed to merge successfully.
Merge Structure Summary Validation Report - Lists items with unresolved conflicts or errors after validation rules have been run using Merge Structure Summary with Validation.
Summaries of mergeable items
You can produce concurrent engineering summaries of mergeable items before merging a lower configuration or project to a higher configuration and use a specific grouping object to narrow the results.
Mergeable Item Summary – Lists all the items in a lower configuration or project that are ready to be merged to a higher configuration or plant, including any items being deleted or terminated.
Merge Structure Summary with Validation – Lists items using a grouping object to specify the data structure to be merged to a higher configuration or plant, and lists the validation results in a report.
For more information, see Mergeable items summary and Client APIs.
Query across multiple configurations
The null configuration is used if you have multiple query scopes selected and you only want to see the administration data returned a single time.
Query behavior affects the shortcut menu commands and relationship expansions. The context of the query, in relation to where the object actually lives, governs which updating methods are available and the results of any relationship
expansion.
Therefore, the result of the relationship expansion depends on the context in which you found the data.
It is possible to query across multiple projects in a single query.
When setting the configurations for the query, you can specify to retrieve objects that are claimed only into the project, or retrieve objects that satisfy the query at the as built level.
The results of the query identify where the object resides. This is especially important where an item may be under change in more than one project. This query context is available on each object that is returned to the client. This
information is available in the detail form, as a column in the list view, or in window titles when multiple windows are used to separate the results of each query.
The context of the query is used for all command access evaluation and relationship expansion. If you query for items in two projects, but only have update access to one project, you can update only the items in that project. Also
when you navigate relationships, only items related in the project used in the query are returned. This is especially important when navigating relationships from a plant item that may have related items in many projects.
Examples of relationship expansion behavior
When viewing objects in the various configurations, it is important to remember that you can see objects in the configuration or configurations set as your current query scope and items at the higher configuration, but you cannot see
objects in a lower configuration. In other words, queries run at the plant level return objects at the plant level. However, queries run at the project level return objects in that project and in the plant above it.
Because of this, the results of relationship expansion can vary when working in different configurations with claimed items. For example, a plant contains a Tag that is related to System 1, which was created at the plant level, but the Tag
has also been claimed down into a project.
If you find the Tag while in the plant and expand the relationship, you see only the original System 1 at the plant level. However, if you change your query scope to the project, when you find the tag and expand the relationship, you see
only the claimed System 1. This is because from the project, you can find items (the tag) at that configuration and higher, but when items exist in both (System 1), you will see the version of the item most specific to your query scope (the
project).
In another example, the plant contains the same Tag and System 1. However, System 2 has been created in the project and is also related to the Tag in System 1.
In this case, when you find the tag in the plant and expand the relationship, you see only System 1 – the relationship at the current level. However, if you change your query scope to cover the project, when you find the tag and expand the
relationship, you see the relationships to both System 1 and System 2. The first relationship exists at the plant level, and the second relationship exists in the project, so both relationships are visible from the project.
How to Configure Concurrent Engineering
This section covers how to configure various concurrent engineering features.
Working with configuration special behaviors
Configuring system and user preferences
Locking data
Configuring a configuration below a project
Configuring an alternate Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)
Making a class definition configuration-dependent
Making an item mergeable
Making a document mergeable
Merging related items
Configuring merge by grouping
Making an item claimable
Managing claims
Configuring revision schemes
Configuring relationship definitions
Creating configurations
Configuring the retrieval of data
Reporting
Configuring notifications
The delivered Vendor Document class is a revisable classified document that supports document reservation so this is used as the example in these sections of this document. Each section builds on the previous section.
Working with configuration special behaviors
Many configurable objects have special behaviors to support configuration management. These special behaviors are summarized in the relationship definition behavior rules section. For more information, see Relationship definition
behavior rules.
Query configuration and relationship expansion
Each object is retrieved in the context of a configuration, which determines the behavior of the relationship expansion.
Objects with no query configuration are expanded within each of the selected query configurations.
This is useful to show different project's specific documentation related to a contractor.
This is not as useful if expanding to other configuration independent data. To prevent multiple expansions to the same object, the relationship definition can be configured to be always handled as configuration independent, and
so can be expanded only a single time.
Query methods
Similar to relationship expansion, queries are performed in each query configuration.
Method arguments can be used to force a single null configuration query, for example, for administration items. For more information, see Client APIs.
Configurable items
Objects that make up the hierarchy are created within a parent configuration, for example, a project in a plant. Roles assigned in the plant govern user access to all of its projects, but roles assigned to the projects are also taken into
account.
The shortcut menu options are evaluated based on user access to the configuration (the plant) and the item (the project).
All relationship expansions are performed in the context of the item. For example, the expansion of the project to a document is in the context of the project, not its plant.
Configuring system and user preferences
This section covers the configuration of any system or user preferences.
Optimistic and pessimistic operations
System-wide options for claiming
Force exclusive claim
Force explicit claim (before edit)
Change user preferences for a claim
Use concurrent engineering-specific object icons
Optimistic and pessimistic operations
Concurrent engineering or long term transactions can be performed in a pessimistic or optimistic mode. These modes can be set across the entire system or set against a specific project.
Pessimistic mode - Only one project can make a change to as-built data at any one time, because this is the only way that you can guarantee data integrity. Data items and documents are claimed exclusively into one project before
modification and cannot be altered by another project or at the plant level.
Optimistic mode - Many projects can work with the same data. The term optimistic refers to the success or not, of consistently merging data back to the as-built level without loss of data from previous project changes. Items are no longer
exclusively claimed into one project at a time.
 Other users can still edit the original records, as the user's changes are isolated, and also make changes at the higher configuration plant level.
There are many examples of when it is necessary to work in the optimistic mode. For example:
General Arrangement diagrams that are shared between multiple projects, where more than one project may change this reference data.
Large equipment, such as towers, which may well have more than one project modifying it at a given time.
Lines, which are also frequently modified by more than one project at a time.
Junction boxes, in which multiple projects may be modifying connections.
Features in both modes
Plant items under change in a project are clearly marked as under change, and this information is available as a column in the list view. Additional information is available in the form of item details or item reports, such as a list of all
lower configurations to which it has been claimed.
A report is available to see which plant items are under change and in which project to review future changes to the plant item.
A similar report shows the differences between the current plant version and the future project version.
Additionally, the following features are provided for managing the optimistic mode of operation, although to insure rigorous data integrity, they are also available in the pessimistic mode of operation.
On merge, the system determines if the design basis of the project has been superseded, for example, by a merge of another overlapping project. If the answer is yes, then you are presented with an interface to help you address the
data mismatches created by the other merge operation.
When a change is made that causes a project design basis to be superseded, the users working in that project are notified.
Any items that are superseded in this way are clearly identified to the users and reports show the differences between the item versions in the project and plant. This report can be run to identify differences between single selected
items or at the project level to identify all superseded items.
When an item is superseded in this way, it is possible for you to resolve the conflicts without actually merging it. This means that the project item is now considered to be consistent with the current plant version and so does not show
up in the reports as a conflict.
Set system-wide options for claim
You can set system-wide claim preferences to force users to claim objects in either the explicit or exclusive mode. For more information, see Configure the system options.
Force exclusive claim
Exclusive claim means that the item can only be claimed into one lower configuration at a time, this also applies to revising across configurations.
There is a system-wide option to force exclusive claim in the system options and another on each user's Change User Preferences dialog box. If either of these are set, then an exclusive claim is enforced.
To force system wide exclusive claim
In the system options, in the Configuration option details section, select the System exclusive claim option. For more information on the system options, see Configure the system options.
To force object exclusive claim
In the Desktop Client, after the first claim of the object into a lower configuration, right-click the object, and click Concurrent Engineering > Set Claim to Exclusive on the shortcut menu.
 To remove the exclusivity setting on a claimed object, right-click the object, and click Concurrent Engineering > Set Exclusive Status to False on the shortcut menu.
 This option is not available for documents.
Force explicit claim (before edit)
The Explicit claim option can also be set for the entire system or at the user.
An Explicit claim means that the user must explicitly choose to claim the object by using a command on its shortcut menu before the user can work on the object. If both of these options are set to false, implicit claim is then in force.
When an item only exists in a plant and you try to update the object in the context of a project, the system claims the object for you and applies the update to the new representation of the object in the project. To avoid any confusion, you
can choose to have the implicit claim confirmation dialog appear when the system wants to create this claim of the object you are changing. You can turn on this dialog in the user preferences.
To force a system-wide exclusive claim
In the system options, in the Configuration option details section, select the System explicit claim option. For more information on the system options, see Configure the system options.
 This type of claim does not apply to documents.
Change user preferences for a claim
 The options on this tab are user-level settings and may be overridden by system-wide preferences set by your system administrator.
1. Click File > Change User Preferences.
2. Click the Claim tab.
3. Update your user preferences.
Preference
Details
Exclusive claim
Forces an exclusive claim. An exclusive claim means that items may be claimed into only one
configuration at a time.
 This applies to both tags and documents.
Explicit claim
Forces an explicit claim. An explicit claim means that you must claim an object into a sub-
configuration to modify it. See Claim an object for more information.
 This option does not apply to documents.
Implicit claim confirmation
A confirmation dialog box is displayed before claiming an object.
This option does not apply to documents.
This is the default claim option if neither Exclusive claim nor Explicit claim are
selected.
Use concurrent engineering-specific object icons
SmartPlant Foundation delivers an alternate set of object icons for use with concurrent engineering. When using these alternate icons, you have a visual indicator that tags or other objects viewed from the plant are claimed into a project or
that the object you are viewing in the project is editable. When working with documents, these icons provide extensive information about the state of the document and location of revisions. For more information about these icons, see
Claimed document icons and Non-document claimed icons.
To use the concurrent engineering-specific object icons, select the Use Concurrent Engineering Icons option in the system options. For more information on the system options, see Configure the system options.
Claimed document icons
The ISPFDocument interface exposes the SPFRevChangedUnderSameConfig property, which controls the icons that are displayed in the client. If the document is revised in the same configuration, the icon is 
 green/yellow. If the
document is revised into a lower configuration, the icon of the revision in the parent configuration is 
 green. This can be changed by swapping the icons.
The icon only changes if a document is revised in the same configuration and it ignores revisions in lower configurations.
The icon changes with any document revision, even in the same configuration. Additional icons identify a higher configuration revision, which has been revised to a lower configuration, and lower configuration revisions, which are the
result of a revise to a lower configuration.
These additional states work in conjunction with the standard document life cycle states, as follows:
<ClassDef>_W - For example, SPFDesignDocVersion_W - Working (standard).
<ClassDef>_C - For example, SPFDesignDocVersion_C - Current (standard).
<ClassDef>_C_SC - For example, SPFDesignDocVersion_C_SC - Current and Source of downwards configuration revise (concurrent engineering-specific).
<ClassDef>_C_RC - For example, SPFDesignDocVersion_C_RC) - Current and Result of downwards configuration revise (concurrent engineering-specific).
<ClassDef>_W_RC - for example, SPFDesignDocVersion_W_RC - Working and Result of downwards configuration revise (concurrent engineering-specific).
<ClassDef>_C_RC_SC - for example, SPFDesignDocVersion_C_RC_SC – Current, result of downwards configuration revise, and source of additional downwards configuration revise (concurrent engineering-specific).
<ClassDef>_CS_SC - for example, SPFDesignDocVersion_CS_SC – Current, with a working copy and the source of a downwards configuration revise (concurrent engineering-specific).
<ClassDef>_SS_SC - for example, SPFDesignDocVersion_SS_SC – Superseded and the source of a downwards configuration revise (concurrent engineering-specific).
<ClassDef>_W_SC - for example, SPFDesignDocVersion_W_SC – Working and the source of a downwards configuration revise (concurrent engineering-specific).
The ISPFDocumentRevision interface also exposes the property SPFRevisedToConfigs, and in conjunction with the property SPFOriginAtHigherConfig on ISPFMergableItem, further icon granularity can be controlled to easily distinguish
claim states on both higher and lower configuration items:
Description
Icon
Notes
Working document with no claim state.
Current document with no claim state.
The Working revision was signed off.
Current document with working revision at same configuration.
Revision A and B in same configuration.
Superseded document.
When a subsequent revision is signed off.
Current document, which has been revised to a lower configuration.
Revision B in lower configuration.
Current document with a working revision at same configuration and additional revisions at lower configuration.
Revision A revised to a lower configuration and subsequently
within the same configuration.
Superseded document, which has also previously been revised to a lower configuration.
Revision A revised to a lower configuration and subsequently
revised and signed off within the same configuration.
Working revision, which is the design basis for a revision at a lower configuration.
Revision A revised to lower configuration and subsequently
revised within the same configuration. Resolve Conflicts at lower
configuration will cause the working plant revision to be the
design basis.
Working revision at lower configuration.
The result of revising Revision A from a higher configuration to a
lower configuration.
Current revision at lower configuration.
The result of revising Revision A from a higher configuration to a
lower configuration.
Current revision at lower configuration, which has subsequently been revised to a third level configuration
Where three configuration tiers are used, the second level
current revision indicates it is both the result of a vertical revise
operation, and the source of a subsequent one.
 To use these concurrent engineering-specific icons rather than the standard icons, select the Use Concurrent Engineering Icons option on the system options. For more information on the system options, see Configure the
system options.
Non-document claimed icons
The claimed icon states for non-document items, such as tags, systems, and so on, are controlled with additional concurrent engineering icon states. These are configured by creating additional icons for a class definition. Since the items
do not typically have other states with another meaning, fewer icons are required. There are four claimed state icons currently supported, as shown in the following table:
Icon
Description
Naming convention
Notes
Tag without any claim state.
<ClassDef> - For example, DEVTag.
Tag at higher configuration, which has been claimed to a lower
configuration.
<ClassDef>_SC - For example, DEVTag_SC.
In this example, color coded red to
warn the as-built users that the
data is under change.
Tag at lower configuration, which has been claimed from a higher
configuration.
<ClassDef>_RC - For example, DEVTag_RC.
In this example, color coded green
to indicate to project users that the
data is available to be worked on
locally. For example, DEVTag_RC.
Tag at lower configuration, which has been claimed from a higher
configuration and subsequently claimed to a third configuration
level.
<ClassDef>_RC_SC – For example, DEVTag_RC_SC.
In this example, color coded
red/green to warn project users
that the local data is under change
in a third configuration.
 To use these concurrent engineering-specific icons rather than the standard icons, select the Use Concurrent Engineering Icons option on the system options. For more information on the system options, see Configure the
system options.
Locking data
A data lock is used to temporarily prevent other users or other sessions from updating the data for an object or a configuration. Once the lock is released, the object is available to be edited by other users or sessions.
In order to lock a configuration or an object, you must first configure a custom property, such as ConfigClaimLocked, for the class definition of the type of object you want to lock. Once the custom property has been created and related to
an interface associated with the class definition, you must add it the Update form for the class definition. For more information, refer to Create new display items.
 Specific Update commands with their own forms can also be configured. For example, configure a method for Freeze / Unfreeze Project using the Update client API with a form that only exposes this custom property. The custom
property is excluded from the default Update command, and a different level of user access is set on the Freeze / Unfreeze command.
Once the custom property is available on the Update form, create a condition to use this property and link it to the methods for claiming and merging the plant, project, or object. For example, the condition on Merge checks that both plant
and project are not locked.
Configuring a configuration below a project
A sub-project or change level can be configured below the project level. All rules that apply to working between the plant and project level also apply to the sub level.
To claim items from a plant to the sub configuration, it is necessary to claim from plant to project, then from project to the sub-configuration. This ensures consistency when the data is finally merged back up the configuration hierarchy to
the as-built level.
To configure a sub-configuration below a project:
1. In Schema Editor, create a new class definition and realize the ISPFConfigurationItem interface.
2. Set the Dependent Configuration property on the class definition to SPFProject so that the class definition can only be created in the project configuration.
3. Use the CreateObjectAndRel method to configure the shortcut menu item New Sub-Project on the Project menu.
For more information about how to load this new schema into SmartPlant Foundation, see Loading the extended model.
Configuring an alternate Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)
The Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) contains information about the concepts of the project in a plant, project, and contract structure.
The delivered core model provides the following structure:
Configuration Top
Plant
Project
Doc Configuration (for published documents)
An example of an alternative structure is:
Configuration Top
Site
Plant
Project
To configure an alternate WBS:
1. In Schema Editor, create a new class definition.
2. Realize the ISPFConfigurationItem interface, and set the Dependent Configuration property.
For information about how to load this configuration into SmartPlant Foundation, see Loading the extended model.
 In an integrated environment, the WBS must be configured as follows for SmartPlant Foundation to work:
Configuration Top
Plant
Project
Making a class definition configuration-dependent
The property SPFIsConfigurationControlled, exposed on the ISPFClassDefExt interface definition, determines whether a class definition is configuration-controlled. A configuration-controlled object is created in the current create
configuration and visible from that configuration and its subconfigurations, whereas objects that are not configuration-controlled are created at the Configuration Top and visible to all configurations.
 Items may still be created at Configuration Top with this flag set, which results in a configuration independent object.
Dependent configuration
To create a project, you must set your create scope to the plant level. The SPFProject class definition instantiates the ISPFClassDefExt interface, which has a property SPFDependantConfiguration value of SPFPlant. When objects have
a value for the SPFDependantConfiguration property, they can only be created at that level. In this case, projects can only be created when the scope is set to the plant level, not if the scope is set to Configuration Top or a project.
Making an item mergeable
For an object to be mergeable, it must exist at level 2 in the configuration hierarchy or lower. It is not possible to merge items into a Configuration Top.
For an item to be mergeable it must have a value of True for the property SPFMergableItem. This property is handled by the system automatically and cannot be changed manually. The property is set to True on the lower configuration
item when the following conditions are met:
The item was claimed from a higher configuration.
The item was created at a lower configuration and its class definition is flagged as claimable.
The Prevent Merge Indicator is set from True to False and the class definition is flagged as claimable.
The property is set to False or a previous True value is terminated in the following situations:
The SPFPreventMergeIndProperty is set to True.
The item was created at a lower configuration and its class definition is not claimable.
 The software interprets an uninstantiated property as False to prevent the creation of unnecessary rows in the database.
Making a document mergeable
In order to be mergeable, documents must meet the same requirements as any other item. However, since a document is a three-tier complex object, the interfaces with properties pertaining to concurrent engineering must be related to
the proper class definition. Interfaces must be realized by the revision, as it is the revision of the document that is copied to the lower configuration or merged.
Also an additional interface, ISPFMergableDocumentRevision, is needed on the revision (only because of server code overriding the behavior). This interface is necessary because the revision, unlike other objects, must co-ordinate the
merging of the other parts on the complex object. This interface addresses the differences in functionality between a normal merge and a document merge.
1. In the Schema Editor, set the class definition for the revision to realize the ISPFMergableItem and ISPFMergableDocumentRevision interfaces as optional interfaces.
2. Load the updated schema into SmartPlant Foundation.
 The ISPFMergableDocumentRevision is deprecated.
Merging related items
You can control if a related item needs to be merged when it is linked to a merging item through a required terminated relationship. This is done by setting the argument Ignore Required Rel If Terminated on the method associated with
the MergeObj, the MergeStructure, and the MergeStructureSummary client APIs.
By default, this argument is set to False so that existing behavior is unaffected. You can set the value to True if more data than required is merged. This way any related items linked to the merged items through required terminated
relationships are no longer merged. For more information on the method argument, see MergeObj client API, MergeStructure client API, and MergeStructureSummary client API.
Configuring merge by grouping
You can configure the SmartPlant Foundation Desktop Client to merge a selected group of items in a defined structure up to a parent configuration. Methods can be configured that use the MergeStructure client API and linked to the
specific grouping object, which defines the data structure of the merge. For example, a starting object can be a commissioning system, commissioning unit, or area.
Each method configured to use the MergeStructure client API contains several parameters, the most significant of which is the Expansion Graph Definition UID, which defines the paths to use to locate the selected items for merging. For
example, a method can be configured for an area that merges all of the tags in all of the units that belong to the selected area.
For more information on the MergeStructure client API, see MergeStructure client API.
If any items exposed by the graph definition cannot be merged and fail validation because they have unresolved conflicts, the user is notified and the items are displayed in a Merge Structure Exception report. The merge is not
allowed to continue if there are any unresolved conflicts or errors.
If the graph definition exposes a relationship that has been flagged for termination at the project level, the software will merge the termination of that relationship. If merging the termination of the relationship causes a cardinality issue
at the plant level, the software will try to merge the item at the other end of the relationship, even though it is not part of the structure. The method can be configured to prevent merging items outside of the structure, as required to
meet your work processes.
The statistics displayed on the notification when merging asynchronously reflect the number of items that are actually submitted for merge. Any grouping items that are at the parent configuration level, or that are not configured to be
mergeable, are not included.
You can create a summary of items being merged using a selected grouping object to define the data structure using Merge Structure Summary with Validation. For more information, see Mergeable items summary.
Merge by grouping structure examples
Simple graph definition
The following example shows a graph definition structure that has a single expansion from a non-mergeable grouping object to a set of mergeable items of one type:
CommissioningSystem 001
CommissioningSystemTag
Tag001
Tag002
Tag003
 All of the items retrieved and submitted for merge as a result of expanding the graph definition are restricted to Tags in this case.
Detailed graph definition
The following example shows a more complex graph definition structure configured to locate a broader data structure.
CommissioningUnit
CommissioningUnit,CommissioningSystem
CommissioningSystem001
CommissioningSystemDoc
Doc001
Doc002
CommissioningSystemTag
Tag001
Tag002
CommissioningSystemEquipment
Equip001
Equip002
CommissioningSystem002
CommissioningSystemDoc
Doc003
Doc004
CommissioningSystemTag
Tag003
Tag004
CommissioningSystemEquipment
Equip003
Equip004
All object types exposed from this graph definition example are configured as mergeable. If any items are not configured to be mergeable, they would be excluded from this merge.
Where a grouping object is at a higher configuration, a merge from a sub-configuration may also reference the higher configuration item. Items from sub-configurations on a common grouping object are ignored when processing this
merge type.
If the grouping object is not mergeable and is located at project level, it will not be terminated on completion of a successful merge.
Items can be located in the data structure of more than one grouping object. When the first grouping object is used for a merge, all the items will be merged up to the higher configuration. However, when the second grouping object is
used for a merge, the software will not attempt to merge those items, unless the items have been claimed to the project again.
Making an item claimable
An item can be made claimable by ensuring that its class definition has a value of True set for the property SPFClaimableItem on the ISPFClassDefExt Interface.
 This does not apply to documents; they are revised rather than claimed. For documents, conditions can prevent revision into a lower configuration.
Managing claims
In order to perform changes in a lower configuration, items or documents must first be claimed or revised into that configuration. This claim can be performed interactively in the Desktop Client.
When you create new objects, or make changes to existing objects, this is done in the context of a create configuration. Before you can claim an item from one configuration into a lower configuration, you must first query for that item in the
context of the lower configuration. This ensures that you have the most relevant configuration version of the object to work with.
When you run a query, the objects are retrieved into a list view that identifiers the query configuration, and all objects retrieved by this query have the query configuration recorded on the object. This ensures that the object selected for
claim is not in a parallel or lower configuration than the active create configuration.
 You must set the create configuration before you can run a query.
Conditional control for Claim method
The claim is initiated by a multi-select right-hand click method that is only available when the following conditions are met:
The class definition of the object is not configuration independent
The query configuration set for the object (QueryConfig property) matches the current create configuration
The current configuration of the object (CurrentConfig property) matches the current create configuration
The IsClaimExclusive flag on the object is set to False
The class definition of the object is claimable
This method ensures that the selected objects have been queried in the active create configuration, so do not already exist in the create configuration, and have not been exclusively claimed elsewhere.
Before using concurrent engineering, you should be familiar with the following types of claims:
Implicit claims - It is possible to implicitly claim items from the plant into an active project configuration when running commands that update these objects.
Explicit claims - The system can be set up for explicit claiming to force all users to claim objects, manually, before they can be edited.
Exclusive claims - Items can be exclusively claimed preventing them from being modified in another parallel configuration. The system can be set up to force all users to work in exclusive mode.
Non-exclusive claims - When working in a non-exclusive mode, parallel changes can be made to the same item. As a result the design basis of a claim may be changed by data merged from another parallel configuration or a
change made to the design basis itself in a higher configuration. This makes the design basis of the first claim out of date.
Documents
Documents are claimed into a configuration by revising them, and as such they can only be explicitly claimed.
It is not possible to revise a document into a configuration and skip an intermediate configuration on the way. For example, you cannot revise a document from the plant configuration directly into a change configuration that is found below
the project. You must first revise the document from the plant in to the project, sign off the revision in the project, and then revise it down into the change configuration below the project.
 A document must be signed off before it can be claimed.
Implicit claim
The system can be set up to implicitly claim objects. This means that it is not necessary for you to use the Claim command on an object. If an action is invoked that requires an object to be claimed, the user may or may not be notified in
this mode, depending on their settings. They do not see the validation dialog box.
 You can configure the system to require explicit claiming, which prevents anyone from implicitly claiming objects. For more information, see Force explicit claim (before edit).
Explicit claim
In some environments, you might want to prevent users from implicitly claiming objects, which could result in claiming objects unintentionally. By forcing explicit claiming, you require all users to manually claim an object into a lower
configuration before editing it there.
History retention
A merge retains the history of the merge for effective date support only. The objects at the lower configuration are terminated, rather than deleted, which means that they can be viewed by setting the effective date to be before the merge
date.
 To view the history, right-click the object, and then click History on the shortcut menu.
Configuring revision schemes
You can set the TakeNextMajorOnMerge option on the merge method, so that SmartPlant Foundation always uses the next major revision in the parent configuration when merging a document.
 In the illustration below, the document was revised from Plant A into the different projects as separate minor revisions. If the TakeNextMajorOnMerge option is not set on the merge method, SmartPlant Foundation tries to keep the
current revision.
This can be confusing if used with one revision scheme.
Different revision schemes for plants and projects
It is possible to use one revision scheme for plants and a different revision scheme for projects. To do this, create two revision schemes, one for each level, such as the following:
RevA for Config level = 1.
RevA1 for Config level = 2.
 There is no need to set the Take next major revision on merge property on RevA, because the system automatically does that merging from project to plant, which forces a change in revision scheme. However, setting it does
make the intentions clearer.
Different revision schemes for a specific plant or project
It is also possible to define revision schemes for specific plants or projects. For example, you might need different revision schemes in Plant A and Plant B.
1. Create a revision scheme.
2. Relate it to the relevant plant, so that it is used in preference to a default scheme configured at Configuration Top.
 The same options are available for using the next major revision on merge as previously described.
 You can split revision schemes up by configuration, so that only some are available by configuration level or by specific document. For more information, see Revision schemes configuration.
Configuring relationship definitions
For more information about relationship definitions, see Configure a relationship definition.
Relationship definition behavior rules
The following sections define how relationships definitions behave in a concurrent engineering environment.
Relationship ownership
The owner of a relationship may not exist in a higher configuration than the related item. This is because if you look in the higher configuration, you do not see its related objects. For example:
When using transmittals and transmittal sections, the transmittal owns the relationship to the section, as the transmittal is comprised of its sections. If the section exists at a configuration lower than the transmittal, finding the
transmittal in its higher configuration hides the section.
As an extension of this, it is necessary to force items and relationships into Configuration Top when the owner of the relationship is not configuration controlled, for example, an administration document with an attached file.
The administration document is not configuration controlled, therefore the file also exists at the configuration of the document. For example, the document owns the relationship, so the file and relationship are forced to the
Configuration Top.
Set the Force Null Configuration property on the Relationship Definition to govern the behavior when instantiated.
Relationship behavior
There are configuration considerations that govern the behavior of relationships.
Relationships can be created if you first compare the basic configurations of the objects:
The query configuration of both objects match the active create configuration
Or, the items are in the active create configuration
Or, the items are higher up the same branch of the tree and have not been claimed to the create configuration
Or, the items are configuration independent items, so they are visible from the current create configuration and not in a parallel project
Relationships can be created if you look at the ownership of the relationship:
An object is considered a claim owner of a relationship if the relationship definition identifies it through claim12 and claim21 flags. Therefore, the owner of a relationship is manipulated, such that the owner object and its relationships
must always be at the same configuration.
It is important during drag-and-drop operations to be able to match the two objects to the correct ends of the relationship definition in order to read the correct owner flag. This also has to be done for the condition evaluation.
Relationships can be created if an object owns a relationship to create the relationship:
The owning object must be claimed or created at this configuration
The other object must be:
Either in the current create configuration
Or at a higher configuration in the same branch of the tree
Which makes it visible from the current create configuration, but not in a parallel configuration or lower
 If any of these conditions are not met during the relationship creation, an error message appears.
Relationships can only be modified or terminated if they have been previously claimed. Modification or termination of a relationship in a higher configuration requires the claim of the owner of the relationship.
 A configuration-independent item cannot be the claim owner of a relationship.
A relationship can be modified or deleted if:
The two objects and the relationship are configuration independent or:
The query configuration for the relationship is the same as the current create configuration
And the current configuration of the relationship is the same as the current create configuration
Relationship behavior on merge
When a merge occurs, if a relationship to the merging object is not being merged, it is terminated. A cardinality check is also done to make sure that removing the merging object from the configuration does not break cardinality. If it is
going to break cardinality, the related item is also terminated.
For example, merging an object on a workflow results in the workflow being terminated, as the workflow must be related to a workflow item.
Relationship behavior on merge by grouping object
When a merge by grouping object occurs, an additional validation check is done. If any unresolved conflicts or errors are found, the merge is stopped, and the items in conflict are displayed in the Merge Structure Exception report. Any
conflicts and errors are listed in the report for resolution. The administrator can choose to merge data from other structures when objects or relationships from the starting group object are required to be merged successfully. For more
information on the exception report, see Reviewing merge by grouping object errors.
Creating configurations
Configuration Top is the top node of the configuration hierarchy. This top node is delivered with SmartPlant Foundation. Data and documents created here are configuration independent; thus, visible and available for update from any
configuration.
The plant is the first level in the configuration hierarchy and usually contains the as-built data. The project must be created in plant scope. This is a way of forcing a project to be created in the plant.
Create a plant
1. In SmartPlant Foundation Desktop Client on the Set Active Scope dialog box, set the create configuration to Scope Not Set.
2. Click File > New > Organization/Support Items > Plant.
3. In the Name box, type a name for the plant.
4. In the Status box, select a Status (Active or Created).
5. Click OK.
Create a project or a lower-level configuration
 To create a sub-project in the Desktop Client, edit the applicable form (create or copy) for the configuration item to remove the read-only restriction on the display item with the relationship to the higher configuration. Additionally,
you must make the now editable display item mandatory. Once the display item is editable, you can use it on the form to select a project to which to copy the new sub-project.
1. In SmartPlant Foundation Desktop Client, right-click the plant in which you want to create the project or lower-level configuration, and click Create Project.
2. In the Name box, type a name for the project or the lower-level configuration.
3. In the Status box, select a Status (Active or Created).
4. For a new sub-project, select the project beneath which you want to create the sub-project.
5. Click OK.
 Plants and projects can be bulk loaded into your SmartPlant Foundation system using an XML file.
Query configurations
If an object is claimed into a lower configuration, you only see that object in the configuration or sub configuration in which you query it and in the configuration top.
For example, Tag001 is claimed from a plant into a project. If you query in the project configuration for Tag100, you only see the claimed tag. If you query in the plant configuration for Tag100, you only see the original tag. This is
irrespective of whether or not the Query by selected configurations only option is selected.
For separate objects in different configurations, you can control what is seen by using the Query by selected configurations only option. For example, if Tag 001 existed in the plant and Tag002 exists in the project and the Query by
selected configurations only option is selected, a query in the project results in only seeing Tag002. If the option is not selected, you see both Tag001 and Tag002.
 To select all of the projects under a plant at the same time, press SHIFT as you select the check box beside the plant. If any projects under the plant are already selected, then all projects under the plant are unchecked when you
press SHIFT and select the check box beside the plant.
 You always see the objects at Configuration Top as this configuration is independent.
Create configurations
If you select a single query configuration, the create configuration is set to the same configuration. However, if you set multiple query configurations, you must select the create configuration that you want to use.
Configuring the retrieval of data
Controlling Query and QFind
The Query and QFind methods retrieve objects from the database for each of the selected query configurations. Each query is performed separately, making it useful to see the data retrieved by each configuration. This query is not useful
if it retrieves configuration independent objects, such as administration items, multiple times.
A user preference determines if each configuration query is returned to a separate list view or one for all projects.
Method argument 9 on the Menu and QFind client APIs can be set to force a single null configuration query for administration items. For more information, see QFindObject client API.
Controlling loading of the tree view
The Load Object client API has properties to control how objects are displayed in the tree view and how they behave if the create or query configuration changes. For more information, see LoadObject client API.
Reporting
The following reports are available in concurrent engineering and use the ad-hoc reporting engine for their output.
View Future Changes Report - Lists items in lower configurations from the configuration top or plant level that have been changed, as well as the configuration or configurations in which these items are currently located.
Non Mergeable Items Report - Lists items in a configuration that cannot be merged due to design basis conflicts.
Configuration Report - Lists all new changed items, and terminated items within the configuration, as well as their merge status.
Merge Progress Notification Error Report - Lists items directly related to the merge progress notification item that failed to merge successfully.
Reporting merge progress errors
Any items that fail to merge successfully are directly related to the merge progress notification item, if one exists, for easy identification. This relationship is defined using the SPFMergeFailedItems relation definition.
When an item fails to merge, an error is raised and appended to a text based logging file. The information is logged to allow cross-referencing from the failed item related to the merge progress notification item, to the errors logged in the
file. Once the merge process is complete, the generated log file is attached directly to the merge progress notification item for easy access, and vaulted in the SmartPlant Foundation vault.
 The logging text file is initially located in the user’s temp directory on the server and is attached to the merge progress notification item at the end of the merge process. The name of the text based logging file is comprised of the
following values:
The name of the user who started the merge action
The date and time that the merge action was started
The name of the configuration or project that is being merged
For example, superuser-04-14-2014-12:44:30-Project10.txt.
Reviewing items that failed to merge
When merging a complete configuration, the merge action must be initiated from the parent configuration level. For example, if you are merging an entire project configuration to a plant, this must be done from the plant. Any items that are
attached to the merge progress notification item because they failed to merge are attached from the lower configuration or project than the current selected scope. Therefore, to review any failed items that are attached to the notification
item, you must change your scope to the lower configuration or project. You can navigate to the merge progress notification item and expand the relationship definition to view the logging file and see all the failed items shown in the correct
context, otherwise they are not shown. For more information about merge progress error reporting, see Reporting merge progress errors.
Reviewing merge by grouping object errors
Before a merge by grouping process starts, all objects are run through specific validation rules. If any object fails validation, an error is produced and displayed in a list view in the Merge Structure Exception report.
You can resolve the displayed conflicts and errors, so that the merge by grouping object can complete successfully. Any items with unresolved conflicts can be displayed in a separate list by selecting Get Conflicts. Any items that requires
independent merging, due to being removed from the selected structure, can be displayed in a separate list by selecting Get Moved Items.
Items that no longer belong to the merge by grouping data structure, such as a tag on the end of a relationship that has been flagged for termination, should be considered carefully before merging. They are included in the data
structure so that the plant or project being merged is correctly represented. Merge by grouping structure checks for this situation and will display the tag as a moved item in a separate list by selecting Get Moved Items.
A summary of the items using a merge grouping data structure can be displayed before the merge by using a merge items summary with validation in the Desktop Client.
Mergeable items summary
When a lower configuration is merged to a higher configuration, the values and relationships from the lower configuration items replace the values and relationship items at the higher configuration. Any related items may be automatically
merged depending on the relationship configuration in the schema. You can create two summary reports of items being merged using methods that use specific client APIs, as follows:
Mergeable Items Summary - Creates a summary of lower configuration items that are ready to be merged up to a higher configuration (or plant) by creating a method using the MergeableItemSummary client API in SmartPlant
Foundation Desktop Client.
Merge Structure Summary with Validation - Creates a summary of lower configuration items being merged to a higher configuration (or plant) using a specific grouping object by creating a method using the
MergeStructureSummary client API in SmartPlant Foundation Desktop Client. This summary also runs validation rules on the structured items being merged and lists the results in the Merge Structure Summary Validation Report.
You have the option to view the validation messages that Merge Structure Summary with Validation creates. This is configured by selecting True on the Show any validation errors before the summary? argument on the
Merge Structure Summary method. This gives you the flexibility to run Merge Structure Summary with or without displaying validation.
You have the option to export a full summary report to Microsoft Excel that includes the types and quantities of the objects being merged, as well as the modified details down to the property level. The export of the full report is
configured by selecting True on the Export at Property Level? argument on the MergeableItemSummary client API or the MergeStructureSummary API in SmartPlant Foundation Desktop Client.
The export of the full report of merge summary data to Microsoft Excel works asynchronously in the background, as reports for large projects could take a long time to produce. A notification object is created in the Notification
List window to view the report's export progress.
 Refresh the notification item to view the export report's current status.
For more information on configuring each of the merge summaries, see MergeableItemSummary client API and MergeStructureSummary client API.
For more information on creating a mergeable items summary or a merge structure summary and exporting the results to Microsoft Excel, see Generate a mergeable item summary.
Configuring notifications
You can trigger notifications of specific events related to changes to the design basis or parallel claims on an item.
To configure a notification about a claimed object, you must add the events to Argument 2 on the ClaimObj method. If you add multiple events to Argument 2, you must be separate them with a comma. For example, you can subscribe to
events by adding Claimed,Merged,EvUpdated to Argument 2.
 For documents, you can also configure the Revise client API to notify you of specific events. For more information on configuring the Revise client API, or configuring other client APIs and methods for concurrent engineering, see
Revise client API and Client APIs.
Configuring the Merge Progress Notification feature
You can configure the Desktop Client to display the progress statistics of a merge action by using the notification functionality to create a Merge Progress Notification item in the Notification List window. The SPFNotification class
definition realizes the ISPFMergeProgressNotification interface definition, which exposes properties that display the merge progress statistics. The statistics are displayed in the Merge Progress Notification section in the Properties
window, and can be refreshed as the merge progresses.
The MergeObj client API and MergeConfig client API have an argument, Run in asynchronous mode, that allows you to decide whether to use asynchronous mode or not, depending on your merge progress administration needs.
Running in asynchronous mode allows you to continue working in the Desktop Client on unrelated work items, which is useful if you are merging large data sets.
If you are merging smaller sets of data, you can choose not to process the merge operation asynchronously, and the merge progress statistics will not appear in the Properties window.
If you merging large sets of data you can choose to commit in larger sets by setting the Commit Frequency argument to tune performance. The default value is set to 50, but the maximum size is limited to 500 to prevent large
rollbacks and excessive memory usage.
 You can merge all the data in one single large transaction by setting the Commit Frequency value to -1. This results in either the Completed or Failed status, and avoids partial merges displayed with the Completed With
Errors status.
The Properties window displays the progress statistics only for asynchronous merges.
To configure the Desktop Client to use asynchronous mode and display merge progress statistics, create a new method using either the MergeConfig or the MergeObj client API. This method uses asynchronous mode and creates a new
merge progress notification item in the Notifications List for each merge operation. The user performing the asynchronous merge can then click the merge progress notification item and the merge progress statistics appear in the
Properties window.
 The name of the merge progress notification item is comprised of the following values:
The name of the user who started the merge action
The date and time that the merge action was started
The name of the configuration or project that is being merged
For example, superuser-24-14-2014-12:44:30-Project10.
You can use the following steps to configure the Merge Progress Notification functionality in the Desktop Client.
1. Create two new methods, such as Merge With Progress and Merge Without Progress using the MergeObj client API or MergeConfig client API depending on your merge requirements.
2. Set the Merge With Progress method argument Run in asynchronous mode to True.
You can select this method when merging large data sets or projects when you want to continue to work during the merge process, and view the merge progress statistics.
3. Set the Merge Without Progress method argument Run in asynchronous mode to False.
You can select this method when merging small data sets or objects, but you have to wait until the merge process finishes as the merge runs synchronously.
 Creating two methods gives you the option to merge data synchronously or asynchronously, depending on your merge requirements.
For more information on the MergeConfig and MergeObj client APIs and methods, see MergeConfig client API and MergeObj client API. For more information on creating methods, see Create or update a method.
Understanding the Merge Progress Notification information
As the merge action progresses, the following merge progress information is displayed in the Properties window.
Expected number of merged objects
Expected number of merged relationships
Actual number of processed objects
Actual number of processed relationships
Number of failed objects
Number of failed relationships
Merge status
Merge duration
The Merge Progress Notification functionality is optional and available only if configured to by the administrator. For more information on configuring Merge Progress Notification, see Configuring the Merge Progress Notification
feature.
Any items that fail to merge successfully are directly related from a log file to the merge progress notification item for easy identification. For more information, see Reviewing items that failed to merge.
The merge process runs asynchronously with Merge Progress Notification functionality enabled. As the merge progresses, you can continue to work on unrelated tasks in the Desktop Client, which is useful if you are merging large
datasets. For more information on configuring merge progress to run asynchronously, see Configuring the Merge Progress Notification feature.
Configuring concurrent engineering inconsistency reports
Inconsistency reports highlight the data that is different between different configurations in your site. Using these inconsistency reports, you can create a report in Microsoft Excel to see what properties for objects have different values
between different projects or between the plant and the project. See Viewing inconsistency reports in Microsoft Excel for more information about running these reports.
The SmartPlant Foundation database includes the following methods for generating concurrent engineering inconsistency reports in Excel. However, these methods are not associated with any access groups by default. To make these
methods available to users, you must associate the methods with one or more access groups.
InstrumentConfigurationInconsistenciesByDocument
ConfigurationInconsistenciesByDocument
ConfigurationInconsistencies
AllConfigurationInconsistenciesByQuery
CDWEquConfigurationInconsistenciesByQuery
CDWInsConfigurationInconsistenciesByQuery
SharedEquConfigurationInconsistenciesByQuery
SharedInsConfigurationInconsistenciesByQuery
Concurrent Engineering Model
Configuration items
SPFConfigurationTop class
This is one of three classes that make up the configuration hierarchy (top one). The others are SPFPlant and SPFProject, which have a similar structure with regards to the realized interfaces. Only one object of this class is instantiated in
each site.
SPFConfigurationTree relationship definition
This relationship builds the configuration hierarchy.
SPFConfigurationStatus class
This class holds the status of the configuration and is modeled as an object, and not as a property. This object is related to an access group to control the availability of methods and so on, based on the status of the configuration. Most
access groups are related to the Active status, but groups such as ConfigAdmin are related to other states. For more information on configuring access, see Configuring security.
SPFConfigurationConfigurationStatus relationship definition
This relationship definition links the configuration to its current status.
Claimable items
An item is available or applicable to be claimed when it instantiates the ISPFClaimableItem interface definition. The presence or absence of this interface definition is in turn controlled by the item’s class definition, which realizes the
interface definition.
An item is identified as being claimed by the property SPFOriginAtHigherConfig on the ISPFMergableItem interface definition. Additionally, an instance of the ISPFClaimedItem interface definition is created on the lower configuration item.
An item may be unclaimed if it was claimed in error or if the requirements of the design change evolve. The Unclaim command is available based on the presence of the ISPFClaimedItem interface definition.
 Documents are not claimed; they are revised.
Optional claimable items
An optional claimable item is an object that has been related to a master item using the claim12 and claim21 properties on specific relationship definitions. When the main item is selected for a claim, any related optional items are also
available to be claimed. These appear in a separate Optional Objects panel in the Claim dialog box.
For more information on claiming optional objects, see Claim optional objects. For more information on configuring the relationship definitions for a claim, see Relationship definitions.
Mergeable items
Once an object has been claimed into a project or lower configuration and modified, it needs to be merged back up to the plant or higher configuration for inclusion in the as-built data.
Additional properties
ClaimedToConfigs
The ClaimedToConfigs property definition is exposed on the ISPFMergableItem interface definition. This property is also stored as a column on certain tables in the database, such as all *OBJ and *REL tables.
This property is scoped by a string type and is instantiated on higher configuration items at the point they are claimed to a lower configuration. The property stores the UIDs of all of the projects, or lower configurations, to which the item
was claimed. If the object was claimed to multiple projects, these UIDs are stored as a comma separated list.
The ClaimedToConfigs property allows the software to recognize that the item at the higher configuration is under change in other projects, as well as showing the project or projects in which the change is occurring.
When a claimed item is merged or unclaimed, the configuration from which it was merged is removed from the string. The string is emptied completely if merged from the last project to claim it.
The ClaimedToConfigs property definition is:
Visible in the properties grid
Available to be exposed in column sets
Applied to relationships, as well as objects
Stored as a new column in the database for all OBJ and REL tables
SPFRevisedToConfigs
The SPFRevisedToConfigs property definition is exposed on the ISPFDocumentRevision interface. This property is scoped by a string type and is instantiated on any higher configuration document revisions when a document is revised
to a lower configuration.
For documents, the SPFRevisedToConfigs property is instantiated on the plant level revision that is selected to be the source of a cross-configuration revise action. This property stores the UID of the project to which the document was
revised, similar to the ClaimedToConfigs property definition. If a document is revised to multiple projects, this is in a comma separated list.
When a revised document is merged, the configuration from which it was merged is removed from the string. The string is emptied completely if merged from the last project to revise it.
The SPFRevisedToConfigs property definition is:
Exposed on ISPFDocumentRevision
Visible in the properties grid
Available to be exposed in column sets
SPFOriginAtHigherConfig
The intent of the SPFOriginAtHigherConfig property is similar to the ClaimedToConfigs property definition, but this property is targeted at documents. It allows the software to recognize that the document at the higher configuration has
been revised to one or more lower configurations for potential changes.
This property is instantiated on any item that was created as a result of a claim action, therefore it applies to objects in a project and any levels below, but not objects at the Configuration Top or plant level.
The SPFOriginAtHigherConfig property definition is:
Exposed on ISPFMergableItem
Scoped by Boolean
Visible in the properties grid
Available to be exposed in column sets
Document revise and merge life cycle
The system always merges the latest revision and always terminates the objects in the lower configuration. For more information, see History retention.
Initial creation
The following image illustrates the relationships between the document master, the revision (A), and the version (A,1).
Revise in same configuration
The following image illustrates the relationships between the document master, further revisions (B), and versions (B,1).
Revise to sub configuration
The following image illustrates that the second revision (B) at the plant level has been revised down into revision (C) in the sub configuration, and a link is created between those two. The revision (C) is also linked to the master design
basis.
When a document is revised to a sub configuration, the icon from the higher configuration (plant) is updated to indicate that it also has a revision in a lower configuration (project). The ClaimedToConfigs property is set to the name of the
lower configuration to which the revision was revised.
Revise in the sub configuration
The following image illustrates that the second revision (B) in the plant was revised into a sub configuration, as revision (C), which was signed off and now has been subsequently revised to revision (D) within the project.
The new revision (D) in the lower configuration is initially in a working state and has a modified icon to indicate that it is both working and the result of a revise to a lower configuration and retains the higher configuration design basis, from
where it originated. When the lower configuration revision (D) is signed off, it is given an icon indicating that it is both current and the result of a revise in a lower configuration.
If the current revision (D) is revised again at the lower configuration, the next revision retains the higher configuration design basis, from which it originated. The new working revision icon is the same as the first working revision at the
lower configuration and the result of a downwards configuration revision.
Revise in parent configuration/merge from another configuration
The following image illustrates that the document revised to the project (revision C) is merged back to the plant, where it retains a link to the previous plant revision (B), from where it originated. However, once the merge is complete, the
revision (C) in the lower configuration no longer retains the link to the document at the higher configuration, from which it was revised.
 Revisions at the higher configuration display the revised to lower configuration indicator for the design basis revision only.
Resolve conflicts on the latest revision in the low configuration
The following image illustrates that all conflicts have been resolved in the lower sub configuration project level. When the project level revision is revised back into the plant level, it moves the design basis relationship to point to the master
design basis.
Merge the latest revision
The following image illustrates that when lower configuration revisions are merged with the higher configuration revisions, the icons return to standard document management icons. The lower project revisions are terminated and so are no
longer available to be seen in the system.
For more information on terminated items and history retention, see History retention.
Schema extensions
The following properties are modeled in the schema for the specific use of concurrent engineering management, which SmartPlant Foundation extensions use to configure concurrent engineering. Many of these are on administration items
that are detailed further How to Configure the GUI Model.
Class definitions
The following three properties, which can be set on the class definitions in the Schema Editor, control how objects are created or available for claim in the configuration.
Property
Description
Configuration Controlled
Set to True if the objects are to be created within a configuration, such as a plant or project. Set to False for configuration
independent data, such as administration items.
Dependent Configuration
Set if the objects can only be created within a particular configuration. For example, set to SPFPlant for the SPFProject class
definition.
Claimable
Set to True if the objects are to be available for a claim action. Set to False if items of this type never need to be claimed.
To see these properties, select SPF Extensions in the Category box on the Properties tab when editing the class definition in the Schema Editor.
For more information on how to use the Properties tab to update the class definitions, see Create a Class Definition.
Interface definitions
The following two properties on the InterfaceDefExt interface control if an interface is copied during a merge or when interfaces are copied with a claim. Some properties on the object at the higher configuration may not be applicable to the
new object created at the lower configuration during the claim operation. Similarly, other properties applicable to claimed object are not applicable to the original object at the higher configuration once it is merged back. The properties
